title: A Discrete Radon Transform Based on the Area of Cube-Plane Intersection
summary: The Radon transform is a fundamental tool for analyzing data in tomographic imaging, optimal transport, crystallography, and geometric analysis. Numerical computations require an accurate discretization. To deal with voxelized images and objects, we derive a closed-form, piecewise polynomial expression for the Radon transform of an axis-aligned cube in arbitrary dimension $d$. Building on this formula, we propose a discrete Radon transform in $\mathbb{R}^d$ that is both analytically exact for voxelized data and computationally efficient. For improved numerical stability, we introduce a regularized variant replacing the Radon transform of a cube, i.e.\ the $(d-1)$-dimensional area of the intersection between that cube and a hyperplane, by the $d$-dimensional volume of the intersection between the cube and a thin slab around the hyperplane. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in several applications including 3D shape matching, classification, and sliced Wasserstein barycenters. The computational efficiency in higher dimensions is verified by a comparison with Monte Carlo integration.

AI.tldr: A closed-form piecewise polynomial expression for the Radon transform of an axis-aligned cube in arbitrary dimension d is derived, enabling an analytically exact and computationally efficient discrete Radon transform for voxelized data, with a regularized variant for improved numerical stability.
AI.motivation: The Radon transform is fundamental in many fields (tomographic imaging, optimal transport, crystallography, geometric analysis), but numerical computations require accurate discretization. Existing methods need an efficient and exact discretization for voxelized images and objects.
AI.method: Derive a closed-form piecewise polynomial expression for the Radon transform of an axis-aligned cube in arbitrary dimension d. Build on this to propose a discrete Radon transform that is analytically exact for voxelized data. Introduce a regularized variant that replaces the (d-1)-dimensional area of cube-hyperplane intersection with the d-dimensional volume of cube-thin slab intersection for improved numerical stability.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate effectiveness in 3D shape matching, classification, and sliced Wasserstein barycenters. Computational efficiency in higher dimensions is verified by comparison with Monte Carlo integration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides an analytically exact and computationally efficient discrete Radon transform for voxelized data, with a regularized variant for numerical stability, applicable across multiple domains including imaging, optimal transport, and geometric analysis.

title: Efficient Sketching-Based Summation of Tucker Tensors
summary: We present efficient, sketching-based methods for the summation of tensors in Tucker format. Leveraging the algebraic structure of Khatri-Rao and Kronecker products, our approach enables compressed arithmetic on Tucker tensors while controlling rank growth and computational cost. The proposed sketching framework avoids the explicit formation of large intermediate tensors, instead operating directly on the factor matrices and core tensors to produce accurate low-rank approximations of tensor sums. Furthermore, we analyze the computational complexity and the theoretical approximation properties of the proposed methodology. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on four problems: two synthetic test cases, a parameter-dependent elliptic equation (commonly referred to as the cookie problem) solved via GMRES, and a one-dimensional linear transport problem discretized via high-order discontinuous Galerkin methods, where repeated tensor summation arises as a core computational bottleneck. Across these examples, the sketching-based summation achieves substantial computational savings while preserving high accuracy relative to direct summation and re-compression.

AI.tldr: Efficient sketching-based methods for Tucker tensor summation that avoid large intermediate tensors while controlling rank growth and computational cost.
AI.motivation: Tensor summation is a core computational bottleneck in many scientific computing problems, but direct summation of Tucker format tensors can be computationally expensive due to rank growth and large intermediate tensors.
AI.method: Leveraging algebraic structure of Khatri-Rao and Kronecker products, the approach uses sketching to operate directly on factor matrices and core tensors without forming large intermediate tensors, producing accurate low-rank approximations.
AI.result: The method achieves substantial computational savings while preserving high accuracy relative to direct summation and re-compression, demonstrated on synthetic test cases, parameter-dependent elliptic equations, and linear transport problems.
AI.conclusion: Sketching-based tensor summation provides an efficient alternative to direct methods, enabling compressed arithmetic on Tucker tensors with controlled rank growth and reduced computational cost for scientific computing applications.

title: Computing the Gross-Pitaevskii Ground State via Wasserstein Gradient Flow in Diffeomorphism Space
summary: We compute the ground state $u$ of the Gross--Pitaevskii equation (GPE) via Wasserstein gradient descent in diffeomorphism space. We represent the density $ρ=u^2$ as the push-forward of a fixed reference measure through a parameterized transport map $T_θ$, realized by a boundary-preserving Neural ODE. The Wasserstein gradient flow on probability densities then lifts to natural gradient descent in the finite-dimensional parameter space, with metric tensor given by the pullback of the Wasserstein metric. The method is entirely mesh-free and preserves the unit-mass constraint without normalization. We present numerical experiments in dimensions $d=1,2,3$ and demonstrate that the parameterized Wasserstein gradient flow (PWGF) output can be used to initialize the $H^1$ Sobolev gradient flow, reducing the initial energy gap by a factor of $7$ in 2D and $4.5$ in 3D compared to trivial initial conditions.

AI.tldr: Computes ground state of Gross-Pitaevskii equation via Wasserstein gradient descent using neural ODE transport maps, achieving mesh-free computation with automatic mass conservation.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop a mesh-free method for computing ground states of the Gross-Pitaevskii equation that naturally preserves the unit-mass constraint without requiring normalization steps.
AI.method: Represents density as push-forward of reference measure through parameterized transport map using boundary-preserving Neural ODE. Lifts Wasserstein gradient flow to natural gradient descent in parameter space with Wasserstein metric pullback.
AI.result: Method works in dimensions d=1,2,3. PWGF output can initialize Sobolev gradient flow, reducing initial energy gap by factor of 7 in 2D and 4.5 in 3D compared to trivial initial conditions.
AI.conclusion: Parameterized Wasserstein gradient flow provides effective mesh-free approach for GPE ground state computation with automatic mass conservation and serves as good initialization for further optimization.

title: NEP_MiniMax: An Approach for NEPs Based on Matrix-valued Minimax Approximations
summary: We propose NEP_MiniMax, a novel computational method for solving nonlinear eigenvalue problems (NEPs) $T(λ)\mathbf{u}= 0$ on compact continua $Ω\subset \mathbb{C}$. The method combines two key components: (1) a rational minimax approximation scheme where the {m-d-Lawson} algorithm constructs a minimax rational approximation for the vector-valued function from $T(x)$'s split form, yielding a matrix-valued rational approximation $R^*(x) = P^*(x)/q^*(x) \approx T(x)$, and (2) a structure-exploiting linearization technique. The minimax approximation guarantees uniform accuracy while generally keeping $R^*(x)$ pole-free in $Ω$. Eigenpairs are then computed by solving a polynomial eigenvalue problem $P^*(λ) \mathbf{u}= 0$ via a strong linearization that exactly preserves eigenvalue multiplicities. Numerical experiments on benchmarks from the NLEVP collection demonstrate competitiveness with state-of-the-art methods (e.g., Beyn, NLEIGS, SV-AAA) in efficiency and accuracy, with theoretical error bounds directly relating eigenpair approximations to the rational approximation quality.

AI.tldr: NEP_MiniMax solves nonlinear eigenvalue problems via rational minimax approximation and structure-preserving linearization, achieving competitive accuracy and efficiency with theoretical error bounds.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear eigenvalue problems (NEPs) arise in many scientific applications, but existing methods may lack uniform accuracy guarantees or efficient structure exploitation. The authors aim to develop a method that combines rigorous approximation theory with practical computational efficiency.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) m-d-Lawson algorithm constructs minimax rational approximation R*(x)=P*(x)/q*(x) ≈ T(x) that is pole-free in Ω, (2) structure-exploiting linearization converts to polynomial eigenvalue problem P*(λ)u=0 while preserving eigenvalue multiplicities exactly.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on NLEVP benchmarks show competitiveness with state-of-the-art methods (Beyn, NLEIGS, SV-AAA) in both efficiency and accuracy. Theoretical error bounds connect eigenpair approximations directly to rational approximation quality.
AI.conclusion: NEP_MiniMax provides a theoretically sound and practically effective approach to nonlinear eigenvalue problems, combining uniform approximation guarantees with structure-preserving computational techniques that maintain eigenvalue multiplicity information.

title: Enhancement of Proton Acceleration via Geometric Confinement in Near Critical Density-filled Targets
summary: High-quality proton beams generated by laser-plasma interactions are of significant interest for applications ranging from tumor therapy to fast ignition in inertial confinement fusion. However, simultaneously achieving high energy coupling efficiency and beam collimation remains a challenge. In this work, we investigate the enhancement of proton acceleration via geometric confinement in Near-Critical Density (NCD) plasma-filled micro-structured targets using two-dimensional particle-in-cell (PIC) simulations. To optimize laser-to-particle energy transfer, we systematically compared various target configurations, such as rectangular tubes, hybrid funnels, and straight cones. Our results reveals that increasing geometric complexity does not necessarily translate to superior acceleration performance. Instead, the relatively simple NCD-filled straight-cone target outperforms more complex hybrid geometries, achieving a maximum proton cutoff energy of 181.7 MeV and a reduced divergence of approximately $12^{\circ}$ at a laser intensity of $5.5 \times 10^{20}$ W/cm$^2$. This enhancement is attributed to the synergistic effect of relativistic laser self-focusing within the NCD channel and the strong spatial confinement of hot electrons by the conical walls. Furthermore, we identify a unique double-peak structure in the temporal evolution of the electron energy, which serves as a signature of sustained electron refluxing. This refluxing mechanism maintains a robust sheath field over an extended duration, driving the superior acceleration. The proposed target design offers a robust pathway for generating high-flux, high-energy proton beams suitable for next-generation high-repetition-rate laser facilities.

AI.tldr: Simple straight-cone NCD plasma targets outperform complex geometries for proton acceleration, achieving 181.7 MeV protons with 12° divergence via laser self-focusing and electron refluxing.
AI.motivation: High-quality proton beams from laser-plasma interactions are needed for applications like tumor therapy and inertial confinement fusion, but simultaneously achieving high energy coupling efficiency and beam collimation remains challenging.
AI.method: Used 2D particle-in-cell (PIC) simulations to investigate proton acceleration enhancement via geometric confinement in Near-Critical Density (NCD) plasma-filled micro-structured targets. Systematically compared various target configurations including rectangular tubes, hybrid funnels, and straight cones.
AI.result: The relatively simple NCD-filled straight-cone target outperformed more complex hybrid geometries, achieving maximum proton cutoff energy of 181.7 MeV and reduced divergence of ~12° at laser intensity of 5.5×10²⁰ W/cm². Identified unique double-peak structure in electron energy evolution indicating sustained electron refluxing.
AI.conclusion: Increasing geometric complexity doesn't guarantee better acceleration performance. Simple straight-cone targets work best due to synergistic laser self-focusing in NCD channels and spatial confinement of hot electrons by conical walls, offering robust pathway for high-flux proton beams at high-repetition-rate laser facilities.

title: Nonlinear parabolic problem with time fractional derivative
summary: Time fractional parabolic problem for p-Laplacian with double singular Hardy-type potential is considered. Comparison principle and appriory estimates for the weak solutions are proved. Existence of global weak solutions and finite-time blow-up are investigated depending on the optimal Hardy constant.

AI.tldr: Analysis of time fractional p-Laplacian with double singular Hardy potential: proves comparison principle, a priori estimates, studies existence of global weak solutions vs finite-time blow-up based on optimal Hardy constant.
AI.motivation: To study nonlinear parabolic problems with time fractional derivatives and singular potentials, which arise in various physical applications involving anomalous diffusion and singular interactions.
AI.method: Uses fractional calculus and PDE techniques to analyze time fractional p-Laplacian with double singular Hardy-type potential. Proves comparison principle and establishes a priori estimates for weak solutions.
AI.result: Shows existence of global weak solutions and investigates finite-time blow-up phenomena, with behavior depending on the optimal Hardy constant threshold.
AI.conclusion: The optimal Hardy constant plays a crucial role in determining solution behavior: below threshold yields global existence, above leads to blow-up, providing complete classification for this class of singular fractional parabolic problems.

title: Manufacturable blazed metasurface gratings designed by 3D topology optimization model
comment: 15 pages including 8 figures, 1-page appendix including 2 figures
summary: We present the generalization of our FEM-based topology optimization framework to 3D blazed metasurfaces operating in reflection over the visible and near-infrared range [400-1,500]nm. The design region is described through a density-based SIMP interpolation and optimized using the adjoint method, enabling the treatment of several tens of thousands degrees of freedom. A first approach directly applies topology optimization to the 3D Finite Element mesh (mesh-based), yielding a freeform structure that achieves an average diffraction efficiency of 62% in order -1 over two octaves under the targeted incidence. However, such patterns remain difficult to manufacture. We therefore introduce a pillar-based parameterization, embedding fabrication constraints within the optimization loop. The resulting binary metasurface, compatible with e-beam lithography and Reactive Ion Etching techniques, achieves an average efficiency of 57% over the same spectral band in s-polarization, with low polarization dependence. This work demonstrates that large-scale 3D topology optimization can bridge the gap between broadband optical performance and realistic nanofabrication constraints for blazed metasurfaces.

AI.tldr: 3D topology optimization for broadband blazed metasurfaces achieves 57-62% diffraction efficiency over visible to near-infrared range while incorporating fabrication constraints.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between broadband optical performance and realistic nanofabrication constraints for 3D blazed metasurfaces operating in reflection across the visible and near-infrared spectrum (400-1,500 nm).
AI.method: FEM-based topology optimization framework using density-based SIMP interpolation and adjoint method optimization. Two approaches: 1) Direct mesh-based topology optimization yielding freeform structures, and 2) Pillar-based parameterization embedding fabrication constraints (e-beam lithography and Reactive Ion Etching compatible).
AI.result: Mesh-based optimization achieved 62% average diffraction efficiency in order -1 over two octaves. Pillar-based parameterization achieved 57% average efficiency over same spectral band in s-polarization with low polarization dependence.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale 3D topology optimization can successfully reconcile broadband optical performance with practical nanofabrication constraints for blazed metasurfaces, enabling manufacturable designs with high efficiency.

title: $N$-Dimensional curve following for solving numerically systems of nonlinear equations
comment: 25 pages, 3 figures, 1 table. Updated version of a previously published article with minor updates to the Introduction and Bibliography
summary:
This paper presents a methodology for finding numerically, by means of curve-following, all real solutions of a general system of $n$ nonlinear equations in $n$ unknowns, within a given $n$-dimensional box. The main idea behind our method is a) to locate all parts of the curves formed by a selected subset of $n-1$ equations of the initial system, b) follow these parts numerically within the given $n$-box and c) during this process, find all their intersection points with the hypersurface that represents the left-out equation of the initial system. With proper handling techniques, both stages (a) and (b) can be done with safety even when using a rapidly - but locally - convergent method such as Newton's method. Stage (c) on the other hand is theoretically straightforward and can be implemented by examining sign change and using bisection. However, improvement of performance with automatic step-size adaptation is also feasible.
  Since the choice of the left-out equation as well as the order of the unknowns, in some problems may affect the algorithm's performance, a secondary procedure for problem optimization has also been developed. This integrated algorithm has been successfully tested in all classical test-problems that are used in the literature (in total about 130 problems with dimension up to $n=10$) and achieved to locate approximations to all the known real solutions of each test-problem, with a user-provided accuracy. It has also proved to be more than 10 times quicker than Kuiken's 2D curve-following method, when compared in two-dimensional problems.

AI.tldr: A curve-following method for finding all real solutions of n nonlinear equations in n unknowns within a given n-dimensional box, using a subset of n-1 equations to form curves and finding intersections with the left-out equation.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust numerical method for finding all real solutions of nonlinear equation systems within bounded domains, addressing the challenge of locating all solutions systematically without missing any.
AI.method: 1) Select n-1 equations to form curves, 2) Locate all curve parts within the n-box using Newton's method with safety techniques, 3) Follow curves numerically, 4) Find intersections with the hypersurface of the left-out equation via sign change and bisection, 5) Include problem optimization for equation selection and variable ordering.
AI.result: Successfully tested on ~130 classical test problems (dimension up to n=10), found approximations to all known real solutions with user-provided accuracy, and proved 10x faster than Kuiken's 2D curve-following method for 2D problems.
AI.conclusion: The integrated curve-following algorithm provides an effective, safe, and efficient approach for finding all real solutions of nonlinear equation systems within bounded domains, with performance optimization through problem-specific parameter selection.

title: Strong gradient neoclassical transport in the plateau regime
summary: Strong gradient regions in tokamaks such as the pedestal or internal transport barriers are regions of reduced turbulence where neoclassical transport can play a dominant role. In pedestals, gradient lengths comparable to the ion poloidal gyroradius have been measured. Standard neoclassical theory can miss important strong gradient effects in these regions because it assumes that the gradient length scales of density, temperature and potential are larger than the ion poloidal gyroradius. We extend plateau regime neoclassical theory into regions of gradients of the order of the ion poloidal gyroradius to capture strong gradient effects on transport processes in the pedestal and internal transport barriers. The fundamental idea behind our new framework is to keep a scale separation between the orbit widths and the gradient length scales by performing a large aspect ratio expansion. In the plateau regime, strong gradients cause poloidal variation that is in-out as well as up-down asymmetric. We study two different test cases assuming either radial force balance or the absence of turbulence and show that strong gradient effects can enhance or reduce standard neoclassical theory predictions in the plateau regime in strong gradient regions.

AI.tldr: Extended plateau regime neoclassical theory to handle strong gradients comparable to ion poloidal gyroradius in tokamak pedestals and transport barriers, showing gradient effects can enhance or reduce standard predictions.
AI.motivation: Standard neoclassical theory fails in strong gradient regions like pedestals and internal transport barriers where gradient lengths approach ion poloidal gyroradius, missing important transport effects.
AI.method: Extended plateau regime neoclassical theory using large aspect ratio expansion to maintain scale separation between orbit widths and gradient length scales, accounting for poloidal variation asymmetries.
AI.result: Strong gradient effects can either enhance or reduce standard neoclassical theory predictions in plateau regime, depending on assumptions (radial force balance vs. absence of turbulence).
AI.conclusion: New framework captures important strong gradient effects in tokamak pedestals and transport barriers, providing more accurate transport predictions in these critical regions.

title: Conformally invariant equations with negative critical exponents on the three dimensional hyperbolic space
summary:
We establish a symmetry result for positive entire solutions with a prescribed growth rate to the following fourth order equation on the 3-dimensional hyperbolic space $\mathbb{H}^3$: \[ P_2 u = - u^{-7}, \] where $P_2$ denotes the fourth-order Paneitz operator. We prove that any positive solution $u$ on $\mathbb{H}^3$ exhibiting exponential growth at infinity must, up to hyperbolic isometries, be radial and strictly decreasing with respect to some point $P \in \mathbb{H}^3$.
  Fourth order equations with negative critical growth on 3-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^3$ has been studied by Choi and Xu in \cite{CX09 }, and subsequently by McKenna and Reichel \cite{MR03} and Xu \cite{Xu05}. Unlike the Euclidean case, the behavior of the Green's function of $P_2$ is substantially different, which prevents us from using the moving plane (sphere) method directly.

AI.tldr: Positive entire solutions with exponential growth to Paneitz operator equation on hyperbolic 3-space must be radial and decreasing about some point.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to establish symmetry properties for positive entire solutions to a fourth-order equation with negative critical growth on hyperbolic space, extending similar results from Euclidean space to the hyperbolic setting where the Green's function behavior differs significantly.
AI.method: The authors study the fourth-order Paneitz operator equation on 3-dimensional hyperbolic space, analyzing positive entire solutions with prescribed exponential growth at infinity. They overcome challenges posed by the different Green's function behavior compared to Euclidean space, which prevents direct application of the moving plane/sphere method.
AI.result: Any positive solution u on hyperbolic 3-space exhibiting exponential growth at infinity must be radial and strictly decreasing with respect to some point P in hyperbolic space, up to hyperbolic isometries.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a symmetry result for positive entire solutions with prescribed growth to the fourth-order Paneitz equation on hyperbolic 3-space, showing they must be radial despite the different Green's function behavior compared to the Euclidean case.

title: Introduction to the artificial neural network-based variational Monte Carlo method
summary: In this self-contained tutorial, the variational Monte Carlo method with trial wave functions based on artificial neural networks is detailed. Unfolding the historical background we illustrate how machine learning interacts with physics research. The mathematical tools for both, artificial neural networks and variational Monte Carlo, are introduced. We demonstrate, how the algorithm operates with generic examples from chemical physics, including the harmonic, the Morse, the Poschl-Teller and the Yukawa potential, as well as the simplest molecules, the hydrogen molecular ion and the hydrogen molecule.

AI.tldr: Tutorial on using neural network-based trial wave functions in variational Monte Carlo for quantum chemistry problems
AI.motivation: To bridge machine learning with physics research by applying neural networks to variational Monte Carlo methods for solving quantum chemistry problems
AI.method: Variational Monte Carlo method with trial wave functions based on artificial neural networks, applied to various potentials and simple molecules
AI.result: Demonstration of the algorithm's operation on harmonic, Morse, Poschl-Teller, Yukawa potentials, and hydrogen molecular systems
AI.conclusion: Neural networks provide effective trial wave functions for variational Monte Carlo, enabling accurate solutions to quantum chemistry problems while illustrating the intersection of machine learning and physics

title: Regge metrics with enhanced trace
summary: We introduce variants of Regge finite element metrics with enhanced properties of the trace. In particular the trace operator is surjective to a finite element space of continuous functions. Multiplying these scalar functions by the identity tensor brings one back to the finite element space of metrics. The metrics can be based on high order polynomials and be constructed on refinements, such as the Clough-Tocher or Worsey-Farin splits. Potential applications to general relativity, incompressible elasticity and conformal geometry are sketched.

AI.tldr: Enhanced Regge finite element metrics with surjective trace operator to continuous function spaces, applicable to high-order polynomials and mesh refinements.
AI.motivation: To develop finite element metrics with improved trace properties for applications in general relativity, incompressible elasticity, and conformal geometry.
AI.method: Introduce variants of Regge finite element metrics where the trace operator is surjective to continuous function spaces, allowing scalar functions multiplied by identity tensor to map back to metric spaces, supporting high-order polynomials and mesh refinements like Clough-Tocher or Worsey-Farin splits.
AI.result: New finite element metric constructions with enhanced trace properties that maintain compatibility with continuous function spaces while supporting various polynomial orders and mesh refinement strategies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed enhanced Regge metrics provide improved mathematical properties for trace operators, making them suitable for applications in physics and geometry where metric continuity and compatibility are crucial.

title: Inertial-Range Suppression and Ponderomotive Density Cavitation in Broadband Sub-Alfvénic Turbulence under Plasma Sheet Boundary Layer Conditions
summary: Kinetic Alfvén waves (KAWs) are among the most pervasive electromagnetic fluctuations in magnetized astrophysical plasmas, from Earth's magnetospheric boundary layers to the turbulent intracluster medium of galaxy clusters. Their ponderomotive coupling to compressive density fluctuations remains incompletely understood in the broadband turbulent regime. We present two-dimensional pseudospectral simulations of the modified nonlinear Schrödinger--magnetosonic (MNLS--MS) system governing KAW envelopes, initialized with a broadband power-law spectrum ($|ψ(\mathbf{k})|^2\propto k^{-5/6}$) spanning many interacting modes, at $β\sim 0.1$--$0.3$ representative of plasma sheet boundary layer (PSBL) conditions. A fourth-order Runge--Kutta scheme on a $256\times 256$ grid integrates the system to $t = 40$ (normalized), with total energy conserved to within $0.085\%$. The nonlinearity parameter $χ_\mathrm{NL} \approx 0.25$ confirms broadband sub-Alfvénic turbulence throughout. Magnetic field intensity and plasma density develop spatially intermittent, filamentary structures within the first few wave periods, consistent with ponderomotive density cavitation and plasma expulsion from wave-intense regions. The magnetic energy spectra show inertial-range suppression, with a rapid transition from injection ($k < 0.3$) to dissipation without an extended power-law cascade, in agreement with the moderate magnetic Reynolds number ($\mathrm{Re} \sim 250$--$370$) of the simulation and the observationally constrained range for PSBL turbulence. These results provide numerical evidence that broadband KAW turbulence self-organizes into coherent density structures at kinetic scales, and that the spectral character of such turbulence is governed primarily by moderate-Reynolds-number constraints rather than by the wave physics alone.

AI.tldr: Broadband kinetic Alfvén wave turbulence simulations show self-organization into filamentary density structures and inertial-range spectral suppression due to moderate Reynolds number constraints.
AI.motivation: Kinetic Alfvén waves are pervasive in astrophysical plasmas but their ponderomotive coupling to compressive density fluctuations in broadband turbulent regimes remains incompletely understood.
AI.method: 2D pseudospectral simulations of modified nonlinear Schrödinger-magnetosonic system with broadband power-law spectrum initialization (k^{-5/6}), using fourth-order Runge-Kutta on 256×256 grid at β~0.1-0.3 representative of plasma sheet boundary layer conditions.
AI.result: Magnetic field and density develop intermittent filamentary structures within first few wave periods; magnetic energy spectra show inertial-range suppression with rapid transition from injection to dissipation without extended power-law cascade, consistent with moderate magnetic Reynolds number (Re~250-370).
AI.conclusion: Broadband KAW turbulence self-organizes into coherent density structures at kinetic scales, and spectral character is governed primarily by moderate-Reynolds-number constraints rather than wave physics alone.

title: Notes on an intuitive approach to elliptic homogenization
summary: Elliptic homogenization is used to determine coarse-grained properties of materials with features on small scales for heat transfer and elasticity. When microstructural features of a material have rapid, periodic fluctuations, the solution corresponding to a "homogenized" coefficient field closely resembles the true solution based on the heterogeneous material. Most presentations of elliptic homogenization rely on methods from perturbation theory, which can make an intuitive, physical understanding of the homogenized coefficients elusive. In this set of notes, we derive the homogenized coefficients for one- and two-dimensional elliptic boundary value problems based on arguments which are physically motivated, and with no recourse to perturbation theory. Then, we discuss homogenization of the Laplace-Beltrami operator for heat conduction on thin surfaces with multiscale curvature, an example which has seen minimal treatment in existing literature.

AI.tldr: Derives homogenized coefficients for elliptic problems using physically motivated arguments without perturbation theory, and extends to Laplace-Beltrami operator on thin surfaces with multiscale curvature.
AI.motivation: Traditional elliptic homogenization methods rely on perturbation theory, making physical understanding of homogenized coefficients elusive. The authors aim to provide more intuitive, physically motivated derivations.
AI.method: Uses physically motivated arguments without perturbation theory to derive homogenized coefficients for one- and two-dimensional elliptic boundary value problems. Also discusses homogenization of Laplace-Beltrami operator for heat conduction on thin surfaces with multiscale curvature.
AI.result: Provides alternative derivation of homogenized coefficients that offers better physical intuition compared to traditional perturbation theory approaches. Extends homogenization to Laplace-Beltrami operator on curved surfaces, a topic with minimal existing literature.
AI.conclusion: The physically motivated approach offers clearer understanding of homogenized coefficients and successfully extends homogenization techniques to heat conduction on thin surfaces with multiscale curvature.

title: Hadamard regularization of open quantum systems coupled to unstructured environments in the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism
summary: The theory of open quantum systems addresses how coupling to external degrees of freedom modifies observables and quantum coherence, a situation central to fundamental condensed-matter research and emerging quantum technologies. Schwinger-Keldysh field theory is a natural framework for both open- and nonequilibrium quantum systems in terms of functional integrals. However, its numerical solution is limited by a cubic scaling with the number of time steps. This is particularly prohibitive for scenarios with widely separated time scales, as is often the case for system and environmental scales. We consider a damped quantum harmonic oscillator as a toy model to study a separation-of-scales ansatz based on Hadamard regularization. A time-stepping algorithm for the Kadanoff-Baym equations on the slow system time-scale is presented that captures both low-temperature non-Markovianity and renormalization effects arising from the much faster environment scale.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a time-stepping algorithm for Kadanoff-Baym equations that reduces computational scaling from cubic to linear for open quantum systems with widely separated time scales, using Hadamard regularization and separation-of-scales approach.
AI.motivation: Schwinger-Keldysh field theory is essential for studying open and nonequilibrium quantum systems, but its numerical solution suffers from cubic scaling with time steps, making it computationally prohibitive for systems with widely separated time scales (system vs environment).
AI.method: The authors use a damped quantum harmonic oscillator as a toy model and develop a separation-of-scales ansatz based on Hadamard regularization. They present a time-stepping algorithm for Kadanoff-Baym equations that operates on the slow system time-scale while capturing effects from the much faster environment scale.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm captures both low-temperature non-Markovianity and renormalization effects arising from the fast environment scale while reducing computational scaling from cubic to linear with respect to time steps.
AI.conclusion: The separation-of-scales approach with Hadamard regularization enables efficient numerical simulation of open quantum systems with widely separated time scales, addressing a major computational bottleneck in Schwinger-Keldysh field theory applications.

title: A Mixed Finite Element Method for the Dirichlet Vector Laplacian in Three Dimensions
summary: This work establishes the well-posedness and a priori error analysis for the mixed FEEC-type finite element approximation of the three-dimensional vector Laplace boundary value problem subject to the Dirichlet boundary condition. The Dirichlet condition disrupts the structure of the standard de Rham complex, requiring the vorticity to be sought in a non-standard function space to achieve well-posedness. We derive error estimates that confirm the numerically observed suboptimal convergence rates. In particular, by developing a discrete Caccioppoli-type inequality for discrete curl-harmonic functions, we prove $(k-1/2)$-th order convergence in the energy norm on general domains and $k$-th order convergence in $L^2$ on convex domains, where $k \ge 1$ denotes polynomial degrees of the finite element spaces. These results extend the previous two-dimensional analysis developed in [Arnold, D.N., Falk, R.S. and Gopalakrishnan, J., 2012. Mixed finite element approximation of the vector Laplacian with Dirichlet boundary conditions. Mathematical Models and Methods in Applied Sciences, 22(9), p.1250024.]~to three-dimensional domains with general topology. As a direct application, a discretization of the Stokes problem in vorticity-velocity-pressure form is studied.

AI.tldr: 3D vector Laplace problem with Dirichlet BC: mixed FEEC method analysis, well-posedness, error estimates showing suboptimal convergence rates.
AI.motivation: Extend previous 2D analysis of vector Laplacian with Dirichlet BC to 3D domains with general topology. Dirichlet BC disrupts standard de Rham complex structure, requiring non-standard function spaces for well-posedness.
AI.method: Mixed FEEC-type finite element approximation for 3D vector Laplace problem with Dirichlet BC. Develop discrete Caccioppoli-type inequality for discrete curl-harmonic functions to analyze convergence rates.
AI.result: Proved well-posedness and derived error estimates: (k-1/2)-th order convergence in energy norm on general domains, k-th order convergence in L² on convex domains, where k ≥ 1 is polynomial degree. Confirms numerically observed suboptimal rates.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends 2D analysis to 3D with general topology. Results applied to discretization of Stokes problem in vorticity-velocity-pressure form. Provides rigorous theoretical foundation for observed numerical behavior.

title: Reduced One-Fluid GENERIC Closure from Relativistic Moment Kinetics
summary: In this work we derive a reduced one-fluid plasma model from the relativistic Vlasov--Boltzmann--Maxwell system using a moment hierarchy reduction combined with strong-guide-field anisotropic ordering. The unresolved higher-moment sector of the hierarchy is projected onto its dominant slow thermodynamic mode, producing a scalar regulator variable that represents a coarse-grained combination of charge imbalance, pressure anisotropy, and irreversible kinetic production channels. The resulting reduced state vector admits a GENERIC (General Equation for Non-Equilibrium Reversible--Irreversible Coupling) representation in which the reversible sector reproduces reduced electromagnetic field-line dynamics while the irreversible sector governs slow thermodynamic relaxation. Linearization yields a pair of electromagnetic eigenmodes together with an additional real thermodynamic eigenvalue. The fast modes recover the standard gyrotropic cold-plasma response, including familiar limits such as whistler dispersion, while the slow mode drives gradual drift of the effective electromagnetic spectrum and provides a reduced mechanism for variability in relativistic magnetized plasmas with slowly evolving macroscopic equilibrium. The previous 3-field model captured only the thermodynamic slow-mode sector, whereas the fully closed extended GENERIC model also contains the explicit reversible nonneutral charge degree of freedom, whose frozen-thermodynamic limit recovers the nonneutral whistler-Alfven equations as a strict subset. The model is formulated within the GENERIC framework, ensuring consistency with first-principles nonequilibrium thermodynamics.

AI.tldr: Derived a reduced one-fluid plasma model from relativistic Vlasov-Boltzmann-Maxwell system using moment hierarchy reduction and strong-guide-field ordering, with GENERIC framework ensuring thermodynamic consistency.
AI.motivation: To develop a reduced plasma model that captures both fast electromagnetic modes and slow thermodynamic relaxation while maintaining consistency with nonequilibrium thermodynamics principles.
AI.method: Used moment hierarchy reduction combined with strong-guide-field anisotropic ordering, projecting unresolved higher moments onto dominant slow thermodynamic mode to create scalar regulator variable representing coarse-grained kinetic effects.
AI.result: Obtained reduced model with GENERIC representation: reversible sector reproduces electromagnetic field-line dynamics, irreversible sector governs thermodynamic relaxation. Linearization yields fast electromagnetic modes (gyrotropic cold-plasma response) and slow thermodynamic mode driving gradual drift of electromagnetic spectrum.
AI.conclusion: The extended GENERIC model improves upon previous 3-field model by including explicit reversible nonneutral charge degree of freedom, recovering nonneutral whistler-Alfvén equations as subset, and providing reduced mechanism for variability in relativistic magnetized plasmas with slowly evolving equilibrium.

title: On singular $p$-Laplacian problems with discontinuous convection terms
summary: The existence of positive strong solutions to a homogeneous Dirichlet $p$-Laplacian problem, with reaction sum of a both singular at zero and highly discontinuous nonlinearity and of a discontinuous convection term, is established. Locality arguments, based on suitable measure theoretical results (see Section 3), are employed.

AI.tldr: Existence of positive strong solutions for a singular p-Laplacian Dirichlet problem with discontinuous nonlinearity and convection term
AI.motivation: To establish existence results for p-Laplacian problems with singular and discontinuous nonlinearities, which are challenging due to the combination of singularity at zero and discontinuity in both reaction and convection terms
AI.method: Uses locality arguments based on measure theoretical results (Section 3) to handle the singular and discontinuous nature of the problem
AI.result: Proves existence of positive strong solutions to the homogeneous Dirichlet p-Laplacian problem with singular discontinuous nonlinearity and discontinuous convection term
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes existence results for challenging p-Laplacian problems with singular and discontinuous terms using measure-theoretical locality arguments

title: Distance learning from projective measurements as an information-geometric probe of many-body physics
comment: Main text: 20 pages, 9 figures; Supplementary: 4 pages, 4 figures
summary: The ability of modern quantum simulators--both digital and analogue--to generate large ensembles of single-shot projective "snapshots" has opened a data-rich avenue for the study of quantum many-body systems. Unsupervised machine learning analysis of such snapshots has gained traction, with numerous works reconstructing phase diagrams by learning and clustering low-dimensional representations of quantum states. Here, we forgo such representation learning in favour of distance learning: we infer the pairwise distances between quantum states--already sufficient for clustering--directly from snapshots. Specifically, we use a single neural discriminator to estimate Csiszar f-divergences--statistical distances between distributions--in an unsupervised manner. The resulting clusters reveal regimes with different dominant correlations, often coinciding with, but not limited to, conventionally defined phases of matter. Beyond phase-diagram exploration, we connect the infinitesimal limit of the inferred divergences to the Fisher information metric and analyse its finite-size scaling. This yields critical exponents of the discovered transitions and enables snapshot-based analysis of universality classes. We apply distance learning to a diverse set of systems characterised by conventional local order parameters (1D transverse-field and 2D classical Ising models), non-local topological order (extended toric code), and higher-order correlations (fermionic t-J model on a triangular lattice). In all cases, we correctly recover boundaries between distinct correlation regimes and, where applicable, quantitatively match established critical behaviour. Finally, we show that distances to suitably chosen reference snapshot distributions help identify the dominant correlations within the discovered clusters, positioning distance learning as a versatile information-geometric probe of quantum many-body physics.

AI.tldr: Distance learning approach uses neural discriminator to estimate statistical divergences between quantum states from snapshot data, enabling phase diagram reconstruction and critical exponent extraction without representation learning.
AI.motivation: Modern quantum simulators generate abundant single-shot projective snapshots, but existing unsupervised methods focus on representation learning. The authors propose a more direct approach using distance learning to infer pairwise distances between quantum states from snapshots.
AI.method: Use a single neural discriminator to estimate Csiszar f-divergences (statistical distances between distributions) in an unsupervised manner. Connect infinitesimal divergences to Fisher information metric for finite-size scaling analysis. Apply to diverse systems including Ising models, extended toric code, and fermionic t-J model.
AI.result: Successfully recovers boundaries between distinct correlation regimes across all tested systems, quantitatively matches established critical behavior where applicable, and extracts critical exponents through finite-size scaling of Fisher information metric.
AI.conclusion: Distance learning provides a versatile information-geometric probe for quantum many-body physics, revealing correlation regimes beyond conventional phase definitions and enabling snapshot-based analysis of universality classes.

title: Energy Dissipation Preserving Feature-based DNN Galerkin Methods for Gradient Flows
summary: In recent years, deep learning methods, exemplified by Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), have been widely applied to the numerical solution of differential equations. However, these methods may suffer from limited accuracy, high training costs, and lack of robustness, particularly their inability to preserve the intrinsic physical structures of continuous PDE models, such as the energy dissipation property in gradient flow systems. To address these challenges, we propose a feature-based Deep Neural Network Galerkin (DNN-G) framework designed for structure-preserving simulations of gradient flows. Instead of treating neural networks merely as optimization-driven solvers, we employ them as adaptive feature generators that define nonlinear trial spaces within a Galerkin projection formulation.This formulation guarantees semi-discrete energy dissipation and can be naturally combined with energy stable time integration schemes. Several strategies for constructing neural basis functions are investigated, including random features, structured initialization, and problem-informed pre-training. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed method preserves robust energy stability in high-dimensional settings and accurately captures complex topological transitions. With equivalent degrees of freedom, the DNN-G framework achieves higher accuracy than classical spectral methods, highlighting the effectiveness of neural feature representations for the numerical solution of partial differential equations.

AI.tldr: A feature-based Deep Neural Network Galerkin (DNN-G) framework for structure-preserving simulations of gradient flows that uses neural networks as adaptive feature generators within Galerkin formulation, ensuring energy dissipation and outperforming classical spectral methods.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning methods like PINNs have limitations: limited accuracy, high training costs, lack of robustness, and inability to preserve intrinsic physical structures like energy dissipation in gradient flow systems.
AI.method: Proposes DNN-G framework where neural networks serve as adaptive feature generators defining nonlinear trial spaces within Galerkin projection formulation. Investigates neural basis construction strategies including random features, structured initialization, and problem-informed pre-training.
AI.result: Method preserves robust energy stability in high-dimensional settings, accurately captures complex topological transitions, and achieves higher accuracy than classical spectral methods with equivalent degrees of freedom.
AI.conclusion: The DNN-G framework effectively addresses limitations of existing deep learning methods for PDEs by ensuring structure preservation and demonstrating the effectiveness of neural feature representations for numerical solution of partial differential equations.

title: Long lasting plasma density structures utilizing tailored density profiles
summary: Using fully kinetic Particle In Cell simulations, we investigate the stability and performance of autoresonant plasma beat wave excitation in plasmas with tailored density profiles. We show that a prescribed spatial variation of the background density sustains continuous phase locking between the driving laser beat and the excited plasma mode, thereby enabling precise control of the plasma wave packet shape and group velocity and providing an alternative to frequency chirping of the drive lasers. The density gradient scale is found to govern the nonlinear autoresonant growth, and the attainable saturation amplitude can exceed the classical Rosenbluth Liu prediction and, for appropriate laser intensities, approach the nonrelativistic wave breaking limit. We show that a four laser configuration in a steep parabolic density profile can generate a specially confined two phase quasiperiodic plasma lattice. The generation of such structures may lead to novel applications in plasma photonics.

AI.tldr: Autoresonant plasma beat wave excitation using tailored density profiles enables precise control of plasma wave packets without laser frequency chirping, achieving amplitudes exceeding classical predictions and approaching wave breaking limits.
AI.motivation: To develop alternative methods for controlling plasma wave excitation without requiring frequency chirping of drive lasers, enabling more precise manipulation of plasma wave packet properties and exploring novel plasma structures.
AI.method: Using fully kinetic Particle In Cell simulations to investigate autoresonant plasma beat wave excitation in plasmas with tailored density profiles, specifically examining how prescribed spatial density variations sustain phase locking between driving laser beat and excited plasma mode.
AI.result: Density gradient scale governs nonlinear autoresonant growth; attainable saturation amplitude can exceed classical Rosenbluth Liu prediction and approach nonrelativistic wave breaking limit. A four laser configuration in steep parabolic density profile generates specially confined two phase quasiperiodic plasma lattice.
AI.conclusion: Tailored density profiles provide effective alternative to laser frequency chirping for controlling plasma wave excitation, enabling generation of novel plasma structures with potential applications in plasma photonics.

title: Global Well-Posedness for the Fourth-Order Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation with Potential in the Energy-Critical Case
summary: We consider the defocusing fourth-order nonlinear Schrödinger equation with potential \[ i\partial_t u + Δ^2 u + Vu + λ|u|^{p-1}u = 0 \qquad (x \in \mathbb{R}^n,\ t \in \mathbb{R}), \] in dimensions $n \ge 5$. In the energy-critical case $p = \frac{n+4}{n-4}$, under suitable assumptions on a radial real-valued potential $V$, we prove global well-posedness for radial initial data in $H^2(\mathbb{R}^n)$. We also show that every such solution scatters in $H^2$ to a free solution of the biharmonic Schrödinger equation. The proof relies on Strichartz estimates for fourth-order Schrödinger operators with potential, equivalence of Sobolev norms associated with $Δ^2+V$ and $Δ^2$, boundedness of wave operators, perturbative stability theory, and a Morawetz-type estimate adapted to the presence of a potential. This extends earlier results for the case without potential to a class of radial potentials.

AI.tldr: Global well-posedness and scattering for defocusing fourth-order NLS with potential in dimensions n≥5, energy-critical case, radial data.
AI.motivation: Extend previous results for fourth-order NLS without potential to include radial potentials, establishing global well-posedness and scattering behavior in the energy-critical regime.
AI.method: Uses Strichartz estimates for fourth-order Schrödinger operators with potential, equivalence of Sobolev norms for Δ²+V and Δ², boundedness of wave operators, perturbative stability theory, and a Morawetz-type estimate adapted for potentials.
AI.result: Proves global well-posedness for radial initial data in H²(ℝⁿ) and shows that every such solution scatters in H² to a free solution of the biharmonic Schrödinger equation.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends earlier potential-free results to a class of radial potentials, establishing robust scattering theory for fourth-order NLS with potential in energy-critical case.

title: Adaptive tensor train metadynamics for high-dimensional free energy exploration
summary: A key challenge for molecular dynamics simulations is efficient exploration of free energy landscapes over relevant collective variables (CV). Common methods for enhancing sampling become prohibitively inefficient beyond only a few CVs; in the case of the widely-used metadynamics method, the computational cost of evaluating and storing the bias potential grows exponentially with the number of dimensions. Here, we introduce TT-Metadynamics, in which the accumulated sum of Gaussian functions in the original metadynamics method is periodically compressed into a low-rank tensor train (TT) representation. The TT enables efficient memory use and prevents the computational cost of evaluating the bias potential from increasing with simulation time. We present a "sketching" algorithm that allows us to construct the TT with linear scaling in the number of CVs. Applied to benchmark systems with up to 14 CVs, the accuracy of TT-Metadynamics matches or exceeds that of standard metadynamics in long simulations, particularly in systems with high barriers. These results establish TT-Metadynamics as a scalable and effective method for computing free energies that are functions of several CVs.

AI.tldr: TT-Metadynamics uses tensor train compression to make metadynamics scalable to many collective variables, overcoming exponential cost growth.
AI.motivation: Current metadynamics methods become prohibitively inefficient beyond a few collective variables due to exponential growth in computational cost and memory requirements for evaluating and storing bias potentials.
AI.method: Periodically compress accumulated Gaussian functions from metadynamics into low-rank tensor train (TT) representation using a "sketching" algorithm that scales linearly with number of CVs.
AI.result: Applied to systems with up to 14 CVs, TT-Metadynamics matches or exceeds standard metadynamics accuracy in long simulations, especially for systems with high energy barriers.
AI.conclusion: TT-Metadynamics establishes a scalable and effective method for computing free energies as functions of several collective variables.

title: A layer stripping method for numerical solution of the GPR problem in a layered medium
summary: A numerical method for processing the data of ground penetrating radars for a piece-wise continuous layered medium is proposed. The method combines the layer stripping technique with numerical continuation of data into the complex frequency's domain. The accuracy of the method is analyzed. Error estimates are obtained and numerical testing is performed. They demonstrate numerical efficiency of the method under certain assumptions such as: electrical characteristics inside each layer change slowly, the thicknesses of the layers are at least of the order of the wavelength, conductivity of the medium is not high.

AI.tldr: A numerical method combining layer stripping with complex frequency continuation for GPR data in layered media, with accuracy analysis and error estimates.
AI.motivation: To develop an accurate numerical method for processing ground penetrating radar data in piece-wise continuous layered media, addressing the challenges of analyzing subsurface structures with varying electrical properties.
AI.method: Combines layer stripping technique with numerical continuation of data into the complex frequency domain, enabling analysis of layered media with piece-wise continuous properties.
AI.result: Method demonstrates numerical efficiency under specific assumptions: slow electrical characteristic changes within layers, layer thicknesses at least wavelength order, and non-high conductivity media.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides an effective approach for GPR data processing in layered media with analyzed accuracy and validated through numerical testing under specified conditions.

title: Near-Wall Pathways of Anomalous Electron Transport in Hall Thrusters Revealed by 3D PIC Simulations
summary: Cross-field electron transport in Hall thrusters is widely attributed to high-frequency $E\times B$ instabilities, yet its net spatial pathway remains poorly resolved. Here we perform instability-resolving three-dimensional particle-in-cell simulations of a Hall thruster using a boundary-faithful and highly integrated framework. The model incorporates a realistic magnetic-field configuration, self-consistent dielectric wall charging, secondary electron emission, Monte Carlo ionization collisions, a self-consistent continuum neutral-gas evolution model, and an open near-plume outflow treatment. From the strongly oscillatory three-dimensional fields, we extract the net instability-driven transport by time and azimuthal averaging of the correlation term $\langle n_e E_y\rangle$ and the corresponding effective perpendicular mobility. The simulations reveal that anomalous electron transport is not distributed uniformly across the channel cross section. Instead, it self-organizes into persistent near-wall pathways connected to the near-exit region. By comparing conducting-wall, ceramic-wall-with-secondary-emission, and open-outflow closures, we show that the near-wall transport topology is robust, while the boundary treatment mainly redistributes the detailed strength of the pathway and its coupling to the exit and near-plume region. These results demonstrate a previously unresolved spatial organization of instability-driven anomalous transport in Hall thrusters and highlight the unique role of 3D PIC simulations in revealing it.

AI.tldr: 3D PIC simulations reveal anomalous electron transport in Hall thrusters self-organizes into persistent near-wall pathways rather than being uniformly distributed.
AI.motivation: Cross-field electron transport in Hall thrusters is attributed to high-frequency E×B instabilities, but its spatial pathway remains poorly resolved despite being widely studied.
AI.method: Used instability-resolving 3D particle-in-cell simulations with a comprehensive framework including realistic magnetic field, dielectric wall charging, secondary electron emission, Monte Carlo ionization collisions, continuum neutral-gas evolution, and open near-plume outflow treatment.
AI.result: Anomalous electron transport self-organizes into persistent near-wall pathways connected to near-exit region, not uniformly distributed; near-wall transport topology is robust across different boundary treatments (conducting wall, ceramic wall with secondary emission, open outflow).
AI.conclusion: The study reveals previously unresolved spatial organization of instability-driven anomalous transport in Hall thrusters and demonstrates the unique capability of 3D PIC simulations to uncover such complex transport phenomena.

title: Vorticity confinement for 2D incompressible flows in an infinite cylinder
summary: We study the confinement of vorticity for two-dimensional incompressible flows in an infinite cylinder. For Navier-Stokes solutions with non-negative and compactly supported initial vorticity, we derive quantitative decay estimates showing that the vorticity mass outside regions whose distance from the initial support grows like $\sqrt{t\log^αt}$ (with $α>1$) or like $t^β$ (with $β>1/2$) becomes, respectively, super-polynomially or stretched-exponentially small. The analysis combines an iterative scheme with an antisymmetry property of the Biot-Savart kernel. In the Euler case, by coupling this approach with a first-moment estimate from [Commun. Math. Phys., 367, 1077-1093, 2019], we recover the confinement bound of [Commun. Math. Phys., 367, 1077-1093, 2019] and refine it slightly: the diameter of the vorticity support grows at most like $(t\log t)^{1/3}$, rather than $t^{1/3}\log^2 t$.

AI.tldr: Quantitative confinement estimates for vorticity in 2D incompressible flows in infinite cylinder: Navier-Stokes solutions show vorticity mass decays super-polynomially/stretched-exponentially outside expanding regions; Euler case yields refined growth bound of (t log t)^{1/3} for vorticity support diameter.
AI.motivation: To understand how vorticity remains confined in two-dimensional incompressible flows in an infinite cylinder, particularly quantifying how vorticity mass decays outside expanding regions and bounding the growth of vorticity support diameter.
AI.method: Combines iterative scheme with antisymmetry property of Biot-Savart kernel; for Euler case, couples this approach with first-moment estimate from previous work.
AI.result: For Navier-Stokes: vorticity mass outside regions growing like √(t log^α t) (α>1) decays super-polynomially; outside regions growing like t^β (β>1/2) decays stretched-exponentially. For Euler: vorticity support diameter grows at most like (t log t)^{1/3}, improving previous t^{1/3} log^2 t bound.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes rigorous quantitative confinement estimates for vorticity in 2D incompressible flows, demonstrating how vorticity remains concentrated near its initial support with precise decay rates and refined growth bounds for the Euler case.

title: Quantum Correlations and Entanglement in Generalized Dicke-Ising Models
comment: Main: 6 pages, 3 Figures. Supplementary material: 4 pages, 7 Figures
summary: Quantum systems inside high-Q cavities offer an excellent testbed for the control of emergent symmetries induced by light and their interplay with quantum matter. Recently several developments in cavity experiments with neutral atoms and other quantum objects such as ions motivate the study of their quantum correlated properties and their entanglement to tailor and control the behavior of the system. Using the enhanced coupling between light and interacting matter we explore the properties of emergent superradiant modes using our newly developed Light-Matter DMRG algorithm with strongly interacting spin chains. We explore a experimentally viable generalization of the transverse Ising chain coupled to the cavity light where it is possible to induce multimode structures tailored by the light pumped into the system. We find a plethora of scenarios can be explored with clear and accesible measurable signatures. This allows to study the physics of emergent orders and strong quantum correlations with quantum spins where the local and long range coupling can be efficiently simulated. We find that quantum spin nematic states with long range order and magnon pairs emerge as the transitions to superradiant phases take place. Notably, we show the cavity field allows the optimization of entanglement between spins for different light induced modes which can be used for quantum state engineering of quantum correlated states. Our methods can be used to model other hybrid quantum systems efficiently.

AI.tldr: Researchers develop Light-Matter DMRG algorithm to study quantum spin chains in cavities, finding emergent superradiant modes, quantum spin nematic states, and enhanced entanglement control.
AI.motivation: Cavity quantum systems provide a platform to control emergent symmetries and study quantum correlations. Recent experiments with neutral atoms and ions motivate exploring entanglement and quantum correlated properties in light-matter systems.
AI.method: Developed Light-Matter DMRG algorithm to study strongly interacting spin chains coupled to cavity light. Explored generalized transverse Ising chain coupled to cavity where multimode structures can be tailored by pumped light.
AI.result: Found quantum spin nematic states with long-range order and magnon pairs emerge during superradiant phase transitions. Cavity field optimizes entanglement between spins for different light-induced modes, enabling quantum state engineering of correlated states.
AI.conclusion: The approach allows efficient simulation of emergent orders and strong quantum correlations in quantum spin systems, with methods applicable to other hybrid quantum systems. Clear experimental signatures make scenarios viable for testing.

title: Tensorial Reduced-Order Models for Parametric Coupled Reaction-Diffusion Systems: Application to Brain Tumor Growth Modeling
summary: We construct efficient surrogate models for parametric forward operators arising in brain tumor growth simulations, governed by coupled semilinear parabolic reaction-diffusion systems on heterogeneous two- and three-dimensional domains. We consider two models of increasing complexity: a scalar single-species formulation and a six-state, nine-parameter multi-species go-or-grow model. The governing equations are discretized using a finite volume method and integrated in time via an operator-splitting strategy. We develop tensorial reduced-order model (TROM) surrogates based on the Higher-Order Singular Value Decomposition in Tucker format and the Tensor Train decomposition, each in intrusive and non-intrusive variants. The models are compared against a classical proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) ROM baseline. Numerical experiments with up to $m=9$ model parameters demonstrate speedups of $85\times$-$120\times$ relative to the full-order solver while maintaining excellent accuracy, establishing tensorial surrogates as a rigorous and efficient computational foundation for many-query workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper develops efficient tensor-based surrogate models for brain tumor growth simulations using reduced-order modeling techniques to achieve 85-120x speedups while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Brain tumor growth simulations involve complex parametric forward operators governed by semilinear parabolic reaction-diffusion systems, which are computationally expensive for many-query workflows requiring repeated simulations with different parameters.
AI.method: Two tumor growth models (scalar single-species and six-state multi-species) are discretized using finite volume method with operator-splitting time integration. Tensorial reduced-order models (TROMs) are developed using Higher-Order SVD in Tucker format and Tensor Train decomposition, with both intrusive and non-intrusive variants, compared against classical POD ROM baseline.
AI.result: Numerical experiments with up to 9 model parameters demonstrate speedups of 85-120x relative to the full-order solver while maintaining excellent accuracy, establishing tensorial surrogates as efficient computational tools.
AI.conclusion: Tensorial surrogate models provide a rigorous and efficient computational foundation for many-query workflows in brain tumor growth simulations, significantly reducing computational costs while preserving accuracy.

title: A proof-of-concept for automated AI-driven stellarator coil optimization with in-the-loop finite-element calculations
summary: Finding feasible coils for stellarator fusion devices is a critical challenge of realizing this concept for future power plants. Years of research work can be put into the design of even a single reactor-scale stellarator design. To rapidly speed up and automate the workflow of designing stellarator coils, we have designed an end-to-end ``runner'' for performing stellarator coil optimization. The entirety of pre and post-processing steps have been automated; the user specifies only a few basic input parameters, and final coil solutions are updated on an open-source leaderboard. Two policies are available for performing non-stop automated coil optimizations through a genetic algorithm or a context-aware LLM. Lastly, we construct a novel in-the-loop optimization of Von Mises stresses in the coils, opening up important future capabilities for in-the-loop finite-element calculations.

AI.tldr: Automated end-to-end runner for stellarator coil optimization with genetic algorithms and LLM policies, featuring automated pre/post-processing and novel Von Mises stress optimization.
AI.motivation: Current stellarator coil design is extremely time-consuming (years per design), requiring automation to speed up workflow and enable rapid exploration of reactor-scale designs for fusion power plants.
AI.method: Developed an end-to-end runner with automated pre/post-processing; users specify basic parameters, system performs non-stop optimization via genetic algorithm or context-aware LLM; includes novel in-the-loop Von Mises stress optimization for coils.
AI.result: Created automated system that updates final coil solutions on open-source leaderboard; enables continuous optimization through two policies; introduces capability for in-the-loop finite-element calculations via Von Mises stress optimization.
AI.conclusion: The automated runner significantly accelerates stellarator coil design workflow, enabling rapid exploration of feasible coil configurations with integrated stress analysis, advancing practical realization of stellarator fusion devices.

title: Dimension reduction of fractional Sobolev seminorms on thin domains
summary: We study the asymptotic behaviour of Gagliardo seminorms in $H^s$ defined on thin films $Ω_\e=ω\times(0,\e)$. The first relevant order is $\e^{1-2s}$, at which the corresponding limit captures the vertical fractional oscillations through one-dimensional sections. The second relevant order produces dimension-reduction regimes that undergo a qualitative transition at the critical exponent $s=\tfrac12$. For $s<\tfrac12$, the dominant contribution is driven by interactions at finite planar distance, and the dimension-reduction scale is $\e^2$. In this regime, the limit is a lower-dimensional \emph{fractional} energy with an effective gain of $\tfrac12$ in the differentiability index. At the critical exponent $s=1/2$, the dimension-reduction scale is $\e^{2}|\log\e|$, and the limit is {\em local}, with dominant interactions at scales between $\e$ and $1$, producing a Dirichlet-type limit on $ω$. For $s>\tfrac12$, the dominant contribution is instead driven by interactions at distances of order $\varepsilon$, the dimension-reduction scale is $\e^{3-2s}$, and the second-order $Γ$-limit is still local. We also study the case $s=s_\e\to 1^-$, showing a Bourgain--Brezis--Mironescu-type result.

AI.tldr: Analysis of Gagliardo seminorms on thin films reveals two relevant asymptotic orders: first order captures vertical oscillations, second order shows dimension-reduction regimes with qualitative transition at critical exponent s=1/2.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior of fractional Sobolev spaces on thin domains and characterize dimension-reduction phenomena for different fractional exponents.
AI.method: Study Gagliardo seminorms in H^s on thin films Ω_ε = ω×(0,ε) using Γ-convergence techniques to analyze different asymptotic regimes based on scaling orders and the fractional exponent s.
AI.result: Three distinct regimes emerge: for s<1/2, limit is fractional energy with enhanced differentiability; at s=1/2, limit is local Dirichlet-type; for s>1/2, limit remains local with different scaling. Also shows Bourgain-Brezis-Mironescu-type result as s→1.
AI.conclusion: The asymptotic behavior of fractional energies on thin films exhibits rich phase transitions at critical exponent s=1/2, with different scaling regimes producing either fractional or local limiting energies depending on the fractional differentiability index.

title: A robust high-resolution algorithm for quadrature-based moment methods applied to high-speed polydisperse multiphase flows
summary: A high-resolution Eulerian method for simulating high-speed polydisperse granular multiphase flows has been developed. The governing equations include a compressible gas that is coupled to mass-based moment equations for a polydisperse granular flow derived from the generalized population balance equation. The model includes effects from particle collisions, drag, convective heat transfer, particle-fluid-particle pressure, and finite-size particle force terms. The mass moment integrals are closed using the generalized quadrature method of moments to allow for continuous size distributions. The governing equations are solved by using high-resolution reconstruction schemes and results from decoupled Riemann problems for the gas and particles as each quadrature node. Success of the technique is demonstrated through a variety of numerical experiments including polydisperse multiphase Riemann shock-tube problems, shock--particle-curtain interactions, dust layer dispersal, dust layer dispersal by shock waves, and dispersal of spherical particle shells by high-pressure gas.

AI.tldr: Developed high-resolution Eulerian method for simulating high-speed polydisperse granular multiphase flows with compressible gas and particle moment equations.
AI.motivation: Need for accurate simulation of high-speed granular multiphase flows with polydisperse particles, where particle size distribution affects flow behavior in applications like shock-particle interactions and dust dispersal.
AI.method: Uses compressible gas coupled to mass-based moment equations from generalized population balance equation; includes particle collisions, drag, heat transfer, pressure terms; closes moment integrals with generalized quadrature method of moments; solves with high-resolution reconstruction schemes and decoupled Riemann problems.
AI.result: Method successfully demonstrated through numerical experiments including polydisperse multiphase Riemann shock-tube problems, shock-particle-curtain interactions, dust layer dispersal, and spherical particle shell dispersal by high-pressure gas.
AI.conclusion: Developed a robust high-resolution Eulerian framework capable of simulating complex high-speed polydisperse granular multiphase flows with continuous size distributions, validated through various challenging test cases.

title: Semi-explicit time discretization for linear thermo-poroelasticity
summary: Within this paper, we introduce partially and fully decoupled time stepping schemes for linear thermo-poroelasticity. This means that the mechanics, heat, and flow equations can be solved sequentially. We provide sufficient conditions on the material parameters, which can be checked a priori, guaranteeing first-order convergence of the introduced schemes. Hence, the proposed methods have the same order as the implicit Euler scheme but are computationally more efficient due to the decoupling of the system equations. Numerical examples validate the proven convergence results and analyze the sharpness of the mentioned parameter condition. Further, we compare the schemes with other decoupling schemes from the literature.

AI.tldr: Decoupled time stepping schemes for thermo-poroelasticity that solve mechanics, heat, and flow equations sequentially with first-order convergence comparable to implicit Euler but more computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: To develop computationally efficient numerical methods for linear thermo-poroelasticity problems by decoupling the system equations (mechanics, heat, flow) while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: Introduces partially and fully decoupled time stepping schemes that solve the coupled thermo-poroelasticity equations sequentially rather than simultaneously. Provides sufficient a priori parameter conditions to guarantee first-order convergence.
AI.result: Numerical examples validate the convergence results and analyze the sharpness of parameter conditions. The schemes achieve same order as implicit Euler but with improved computational efficiency due to decoupling. Comparison with other decoupling schemes from literature is provided.
AI.conclusion: The proposed decoupled time stepping schemes offer an efficient alternative to fully coupled approaches for thermo-poroelasticity, maintaining first-order accuracy while reducing computational cost through sequential solving of system equations.

title: Drift-reduced fluid modeling of rapidly rotating plasmas
summary: In this paper, we investigate the effects of rapid rotation (Mach number M ~ 1) on plasma fluid stability, focusing specifically on Kelvin-Helmholtz (KH) and interchange instabilities - including both magnetic-curvature-driven (CDI) and rotation-driven (RDI) interchanges. Building on previous studies of shear flow stabilization, we utilize a drift- reduced fluid approach rather than standard magnetohydrodynamics to capture finite Larmor-radius effects. To achieve this, the drift-reduced equations were modified to include the centrifugal force and implemented in hermes-3 (Dudson et al. 2024), an extension to the BOUT++ (Dudson et al. 2009) framework. Because plasma rotation both drives the RDI and provides stabilizing shear flow, we find that the global plasma stability is sensitive to background profile characteristics. We identify three distinct regimes of RDI behavior and establish a simple criterion based on the density and velocity profiles to predict RDI susceptibility. This approach is similar to recent local gyrokinetic studies of shear flow that compared instability growth rates to shearing rates (Ivanov et al. 2025). Finally, by examining cases where the plasma is both interchange- and KH-unstable, we find that global KH modes make the plasma less resistant to RDI.

AI.tldr: Rapid rotation effects on plasma stability: study shows rotation-driven interchange (RDI) has three regimes, with global Kelvin-Helmholtz modes reducing RDI resistance.
AI.motivation: To understand how rapid rotation (Mach ~1) affects plasma fluid stability, particularly Kelvin-Helmholtz and interchange instabilities, building on previous shear flow stabilization studies.
AI.method: Used drift-reduced fluid approach (not standard MHD) to capture finite Larmor-radius effects, modified equations to include centrifugal force, implemented in hermes-3 (BOUT++ extension).
