AI.result: Identified three distinct regimes of rotation-driven interchange (RDI) behavior, established simple criterion based on density/velocity profiles to predict RDI susceptibility, found global KH modes make plasma less resistant to RDI.
AI.conclusion: Plasma stability is sensitive to background profiles, with rotation both driving RDI and providing stabilization; global KH modes reduce plasma's resistance to RDI instabilities.

title: Sheets of Spectral Data of Stokes Waves in Weakly Nonlinear Models
summary: We study the spectral stability of small-amplitude Stokes waves in a family of weakly nonlinear, unidirectional models of the form $u_t + L u + (u^2)_x = 0$. We introduce a perturbation method to expand the spectral data in wave amplitude near flat-state eigenvalue collisions, with the ratio of the colliding modes as a free parameter. This yields sheets of spectral data whose slices at fixed amplitude give isolas of instability. The same perturbation framework treats both high-frequency and Benjamin--Feir instabilities, extends to discontinuous dispersion relations (including the Akers--Milewski equation), and, for the first time, provides an analytic approximation of the Benjamin--Feir spectrum for this model and a direct comparison of high-frequency and Benjamin--Feir growth rates across the full family of models. Asymptotic predictions are validated against numerical spectra computed by Floquet--Fourier--Hill and quasi-Newton methods.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a perturbation method to analyze spectral stability of small-amplitude Stokes waves in weakly nonlinear unidirectional models, treating both high-frequency and Benjamin-Feir instabilities across a family of equations.
AI.motivation: To understand spectral stability of small-amplitude Stokes waves in weakly nonlinear unidirectional models, particularly near eigenvalue collisions, and to provide analytic approximations for both high-frequency and Benjamin-Feir instabilities across a family of models.
AI.method: Introduces a perturbation method to expand spectral data in wave amplitude near flat-state eigenvalue collisions, using the ratio of colliding modes as a free parameter. This yields sheets of spectral data whose slices at fixed amplitude give isolas of instability.
AI.result: The method successfully treats both high-frequency and Benjamin-Feir instabilities, extends to discontinuous dispersion relations, provides first analytic approximation of Benjamin-Feir spectrum for this model, and enables direct comparison of growth rates across the full family of models.
AI.conclusion: The perturbation framework offers a unified approach to analyze spectral stability in weakly nonlinear unidirectional models, with asymptotic predictions validated against numerical spectra computed by Floquet-Fourier-Hill and quasi-Newton methods.

title: Hierarchy of extreme-event predictability in turbulence revealed by machine learning
summary: Extreme-event predictability in turbulence is strongly state dependent, yet event-by-event predictability horizons are difficult to quantify without access to governing equations or costly perturbation ensembles. Here we train an autoregressive conditional diffusion model on direct numerical simulations of the two-dimensional Kolmogorov flow and use a CRPS-based skill score to define an event-wise predictability horizon. Enstrophy extremes exhibit a pronounced hierarchy: forecast skill persists from $\approx 1$ to $> 4$ Lyapunov times across events. Spectral filtering shows that these horizons are controlled predominantly by large-scale structures. Extremes are preceded by intense strain cores organizing quadrupolar vortex packets, whose lifetime sharply separates long- from short-horizon events. These results identify coherent-structure persistence as a governing mechanism for the predictability of turbulence extremes and provide a data-driven route to diagnose predictability limits from observations.

AI.tldr: Autoregressive conditional diffusion model trained on 2D turbulence simulations reveals extreme events have widely varying predictability horizons (1-4+ Lyapunov times) controlled by large-scale coherent structures.
AI.motivation: Extreme events in turbulence have state-dependent predictability that's hard to quantify without governing equations or expensive perturbation ensembles. Need data-driven methods to diagnose event-by-event predictability limits from observations.
AI.method: Train autoregressive conditional diffusion model on direct numerical simulations of 2D Kolmogorov flow. Use CRPS-based skill score to define event-wise predictability horizon. Apply spectral filtering to analyze scale dependence.
AI.result: Enstrophy extremes show wide predictability hierarchy: forecast skill persists from ≈1 to >4 Lyapunov times across events. Large-scale structures control predictability horizons. Extremes preceded by intense strain cores organizing quadrupolar vortex packets, whose lifetime separates long- from short-horizon events.
AI.conclusion: Coherent-structure persistence governs predictability of turbulence extremes. Provides data-driven route to diagnose predictability limits from observations without requiring governing equations or perturbation ensembles.

title: A Robust Learning-Based Method for the Helmholtz Equation in Dissipative Media and Complex Domains
summary: To mitigate pollution effects in high-frequency Helmholtz problems, Learning-based Numerical Methods (LbNM) reconstruct solution operators using complete systems of exact solutions. However, the previously used fundamental-solution (FS) basis suffers from instability in dissipative media and requires sensitive geometric tuning. In this paper, we propose a robust alternative using a Bessel basis (BB). From a learning theory perspective, the BB forms a complete hypothesis space of standing waves, ensuring immunity to dissipation-induced signal loss. We establish a convergence result that depends on intrinsic regularity. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves machine-precision accuracy in dissipative regimes where FS fails, significantly outperforms the Finite Element Method (FEM) in efficiency, and demonstrates the framework's geometric extensibility via a multi-center strategy.

AI.tldr: Proposes Bessel basis (BB) as robust alternative to fundamental-solution basis for learning-based numerical methods in high-frequency Helmholtz problems, overcoming dissipation issues and geometric sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Existing fundamental-solution basis methods suffer from instability in dissipative media and require sensitive geometric tuning, limiting their practical application for high-frequency Helmholtz problems.
AI.method: Uses Bessel basis (BB) to form complete hypothesis space of standing waves, ensuring immunity to dissipation-induced signal loss. Establishes convergence result based on intrinsic regularity and demonstrates geometric extensibility via multi-center strategy.
AI.result: Achieves machine-precision accuracy in dissipative regimes where fundamental-solution basis fails, significantly outperforms Finite Element Method in efficiency, and demonstrates framework's geometric extensibility.
AI.conclusion: Bessel basis provides robust alternative to fundamental-solution basis for learning-based numerical methods in high-frequency Helmholtz problems, overcoming dissipation issues while maintaining efficiency and geometric flexibility.

title: Investigation of Laser Plasma Instabilities driven by Coupled High-Power Laser Beams in Magnetized Underdense Plasmas
summary: Stimulated Brillouin and Raman scattering (SBS and SRS) are instabilities that affect the propagation of high-power lasers in plasmas. The latter is further affected by Cross-Talk (CT) effects when multiple laser beams are simultaneously propagated in the plasma, as found in the schemes proposed for inertial confinement fusion (ICF). Here we develop a new theoretical model that allows us to evaluate the impact of CT on SBS and SRS in low-density plasmas. As supported by experiments, we demonstrate that CT can lead to a reduction of both SBS and SRS, due to the destabilization of the individually triggered instabilities. We further demonstrate that this destabilization effect is accelerated by applying an externally magnetic field to the plasma, which is also beneficial for the hydrodynamics or fuel heating of ICF. By shedding new light on the promising scheme of magnetized ICF, our findings thus offer beneficial prospects for ICF.

AI.tldr: CT effects reduce SBS and SRS in multi-beam laser-plasma interactions, with magnetic fields accelerating this beneficial destabilization for ICF applications.
AI.motivation: SBS and SRS instabilities degrade high-power laser propagation in plasmas for ICF, and CT effects in multi-beam configurations need better understanding.
AI.method: Developed new theoretical model to evaluate CT impact on SBS and SRS in low-density plasmas, validated by experiments.
AI.result: CT reduces both SBS and SRS by destabilizing individually triggered instabilities; magnetic fields accelerate this beneficial effect.
AI.conclusion: Findings support magnetized ICF schemes, offering promising prospects for ICF by mitigating laser-plasma instabilities.

title: On the Hausdorff dimension and singularities of the monopolist's free boundary curve
summary: The simplest genuinely multidimensional monopolist's problem involves minimizing a linearly perturbed Dirichlet energy among nonnegative convex functions $u$ on an open domain $X \subset [0, \infty)^2$. The geometry of the region of strict convexity $Ω\subset X$ for the unique minimizer $u$ is of central interest. A relatively closed portion $X_1^0 \subset X$ of the domain is comprised of segments starting and ending on $\partial X$ along which $u$ is affine. For convex polygons and certain other domains $X \subset \mathbf{R}^2$, we build on results with Zhang to show that outside $X_1^0 \cup \{u=0\}$, the free boundary of $Ω$ is a continuous curve of Hausdorff dimension one, and that $Ω$ has density $1/2$ along it (and is $C^α_{\mathrm{loc}}$ for all $0<α<1$), except perhaps at a discrete set of singular points. We do this by showing that much of the free boundary solves an obstacle problem whose endogenous obstacle is $C^2$. For Rochet and Choné's square $X=(a,a+1)^2$ with $a>0$, there is a point $x_a$ on the diagonal such that $X_1^0\cap \partial Ω\subset \{x_a\}$, and the discrete singularities mentioned above can only accumulate at $x_a$ or at the two ends of the analytic arc $\{u=0\} \cap \partial Ω$, plus the two limit points of $X \cap \partial Ω$ on $\partial X$. Where the regularity of the endogenous obstacle can be improved to $C^{2,\mathrm{Dini}}$, the free boundary becomes locally $C^\infty$ outside a closed set whose relative interior is empty.

AI.tldr: Analysis of free boundary regularity for minimizers of perturbed Dirichlet energy in multidimensional monopolist problems, focusing on convex domains in R².
AI.motivation: To understand the geometry of strict convexity regions in multidimensional monopolist problems, particularly the regularity of free boundaries for minimizers of perturbed Dirichlet energy.
AI.method: Builds on previous results with Zhang to analyze free boundaries as solutions to obstacle problems with endogenous obstacles. Uses geometric analysis techniques for convex polygons and specific domains like Rochet and Choné's square.
AI.result: Shows free boundary is a continuous curve of Hausdorff dimension one with density 1/2 (and C^α_loc for all 0<α<1), except at discrete singular points. For specific square domains, identifies accumulation points for singularities.
AI.conclusion: The free boundary exhibits good regularity properties outside a small set of singular points, and with improved obstacle regularity becomes locally C^∞ outside a closed set with empty relative interior.

title: Information-Driven Phase Transition on Weighted Graphs with Spontaneous Dimensional Sensitivity
summary:
We study information flow on a weighted graph whose topology evolves according to a spectral curvature measure $\mathcal{R}$. The model (FIU) defines $\mathcal{R}$ from the diagonal of the graph Green function, propagates energy with curvature-dependent dissipation, and creates long-range links between high-$\mathcal{R}$ nodes at a rate controlled by a coupling parameter $g$.
  We report three results. First, the system exhibits a sharp phase transition at $g_c \approx 0.023$: below $g_c$, local information flux $σ$ and structure formation are anti-correlated; above $g_c$, they become strongly correlated (Pearson $r \approx 0.75$, $p < 10^{-38}$), with signatures of a continuous transition and mean-field exponent $ν\approx 0.54$.
  Second, we identify a node-level discrete Poisson relation $\nabla^2\mathcal{R}(i) = κ\,σ_{\rm prev}(i)$, where $κ$ is stable across parameters (CV $= 3.1\%$ across independent configurations). Mediator analysis reveals this correlation is almost entirely mediated by $\mathcal{R}$ itself, identifying it as the central self-organizing variable.
  Third, the Poisson relation exhibits spontaneous dimensional sensitivity: in 2D lattices both signals decay for $N \gtrsim 576$, while in 3D they persist to $N \lesssim 1728$. This emerges without any dimensional parameter in the rules. The collapse mechanism is curvature homogenization at large $N$. We interpret this as topological frustration in a mesoscopic regime, and discuss analogies with dimensional signatures of gravity.

AI.tldr: The paper studies information flow on evolving weighted graphs using spectral curvature, finding a phase transition, a stable Poisson relation between curvature and information flux, and spontaneous dimensional sensitivity in the system's behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand how information flows on dynamically evolving graphs where topology changes according to spectral curvature measures, and to explore emergent phenomena like phase transitions and dimensional sensitivity in such systems.
AI.method: Uses a FIU model that defines spectral curvature from graph Green function diagonal, propagates energy with curvature-dependent dissipation, and creates long-range links between high-curvature nodes controlled by coupling parameter g.
AI.result: 1) Sharp phase transition at g_c ≈ 0.023 with correlation reversal between local information flux and structure formation; 2) Stable node-level Poisson relation ∇²R(i) = κσ_prev(i) with R as central self-organizing variable; 3) Spontaneous dimensional sensitivity where signals persist differently in 2D vs 3D lattices without dimensional parameters.
AI.conclusion: The spectral curvature R emerges as a central self-organizing variable in information flow on evolving graphs, with the system exhibiting phase transitions, stable Poisson relations, and spontaneous dimensional sensitivity analogous to gravitational systems.

title: Inference of interacting kernel in the mean-field regime
summary: We study the problem of reconstructing interaction kernels in systems of interacting agents from macroscopic measurements when posed as an optimization problem. The reconstruction procedure depends on the formulation of the forward model, which may be given either by a finite-dimensional coupled ODE system tracking individual agent trajectories or by a mean-field PDE describing the evolution of the agent density. We investigate the similarities and differences between these two formulations in the mean-field regime. While the first variation derived from the particle system does not provide an unbiased estimator of the first variation associated with the limiting PDE, we prove that, under mild assumptions, the two are close in a weak sense with a convergence rate $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})$. This rate is further confirmed by numerical evidences.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes interaction kernel reconstruction from macroscopic measurements using both particle-based ODE and mean-field PDE formulations, showing their first variations differ but converge weakly at rate O(N^{-1/2}).
AI.motivation: To understand the relationship between two different mathematical formulations (particle ODE vs mean-field PDE) for reconstructing interaction kernels in multi-agent systems from macroscopic measurements, particularly in the mean-field regime.
AI.method: The study compares two forward model formulations: 1) finite-dimensional coupled ODE system tracking individual agent trajectories, and 2) mean-field PDE describing agent density evolution. Analyzes their first variations and convergence properties in the mean-field limit.
AI.result: The first variation from the particle system is not an unbiased estimator of the PDE's first variation, but under mild assumptions they are close in a weak sense with convergence rate O(N^{-1/2}), confirmed numerically.
AI.conclusion: While the two formulations differ mathematically (with particle system first variation being biased), they converge weakly at a specific rate, providing theoretical understanding of their relationship for interaction kernel reconstruction problems.

title: Formation and rising phase of a flux rope through data-constrained simulations
summary: Context. Data-constrained models incorporate observed photospheric magnetic fields. However, due to the lack of magnetic field information in the rest of the solar atmosphere, models rely on extrapolations that, in most cases, neglect the Lorentz force. Nevertheless, this force is present in the lower atmosphere and may play a key role in destabilising the equilibrium configuration and triggering eruptions. Aims. This study seeks to understand and reproduce a solar eruption SOL2014-12-18T21:41 that occurred in active region NOAA 12241, preceded by an M6.9 flare, and to investigate the impact of relaxing the initial force-free assumption. Methods. The resistive and compressible magnetohydrodynamic simulation is initiated using a non-force-free magnetic field extrapolated from a photospheric vector magnetogram taken minutes before the flare. The simulation includes a stratified atmosphere and non-ideal effects such as thermal conduction and radiative cooling. Results. A flux rope forms and rises in the simulation, carrying away dense material from the lower solar atmosphere. Its formation results from the non-zero Lorentz force acting on the initial sheared arcade, without assuming pre-existing flux ropes or photospheric driving motions. The flux rope is then deflected toward regions of low magnetic pressure, escaping the domain at 350 km/s with approximately constant acceleration. Conclusions. A robust numerical framework for modelling flaring active regions was applied to the eruption of NOAA AR12241 as a case study, assuming a realistic non-force-free magnetic field near the flare onset. It exemplifies how an initial Lorentz force imbalance can successfully trigger a flux rope formation that later escapes the simulation domain. It also enables comparison with real observations through the addition of a stratified atmosphere spanning from the photosphere to the corona.

AI.tldr: Non-force-free MHD simulation successfully reproduces solar eruption triggered by Lorentz force imbalance without pre-existing flux ropes or photospheric driving.
AI.motivation: Most solar eruption models use force-free magnetic field extrapolations that neglect Lorentz forces, but these forces exist in the lower atmosphere and may be crucial for triggering eruptions.
AI.method: Resistive, compressible MHD simulation initialized with non-force-free magnetic field extrapolated from photospheric vector magnetogram minutes before flare, including stratified atmosphere, thermal conduction, and radiative cooling.
AI.result: Flux rope forms and rises from initial sheared arcade due to non-zero Lorentz force, carries dense material from lower atmosphere, deflects toward low magnetic pressure regions, escapes at 350 km/s with constant acceleration.
AI.conclusion: Framework successfully models flaring active regions with realistic non-force-free fields, demonstrates how Lorentz force imbalance can trigger flux rope formation and eruption, enables comparison with observations through stratified atmosphere.

title: Existence of Solutions of the third term of the Connaughton-Newell Model with a source term
summary: The Connaughton-Newell equation is an approximation of three-wave kinetic equations using a fully non-linear coagulation-fragmentation model. This equation consists of three non-linear operators. In this paper, we proved that assuming a constant interaction kernel and a well-behaved source term, the third operator of the Connaughton-Newell equation has a solution.

AI.tldr: Proved existence of solution for the third operator of Connaughton-Newell equation under constant kernel and well-behaved source conditions.
AI.motivation: The Connaughton-Newell equation approximates three-wave kinetic equations using coagulation-fragmentation models, but solution existence for its third operator wasn't established.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis under assumptions of constant interaction kernel and well-behaved source term to prove solution existence.
AI.result: Successfully proved that the third operator of the Connaughton-Newell equation has a solution under the specified conditions.
AI.conclusion: The existence result provides mathematical foundation for the third operator's behavior in the Connaughton-Newell equation approximation of three-wave kinetic systems.

title: Nonadiabatic rare events from transition-path sampling of MASH trajectories
summary: Rare nonadiabatic reactions are a key component of many important molecular processes but are challenging to capture with direct dynamical simulations. In this paper, we combine our recently developed mapping approach to surface hopping (MASH) with transition-path sampling to create a framework to efficiently simulate these rare events. This is possible because MASH trajectories are Markovian, time-reversible and obey Liouville's theorem. The combined approach generates nonadiabatic reactive pathways without biasing the underlying dynamics. The resulting ensemble allows for a detailed analysis of reaction mechanisms and the unraveling of statistical and dynamical properties, including rate constants. We apply the method to study a spin-boson model in thermal equilibrium over a wide range of diabatic coupling strengths. Our results demonstrate how this approach provides a practical and systematic tool for investigating rare nonadiabatic processes, potentially beyond the reach of brute-force simulations.

AI.tldr: Combines MASH (mapping approach to surface hopping) with transition-path sampling to efficiently simulate rare nonadiabatic reactions without biasing dynamics.
AI.motivation: Rare nonadiabatic reactions are important in molecular processes but challenging to simulate directly due to their infrequent occurrence.
AI.method: Integrates MASH (Markovian, time-reversible, Liouville-obeying trajectories) with transition-path sampling to generate unbiased nonadiabatic reactive pathways.
AI.result: Applied to spin-boson model across various diabatic coupling strengths; enables detailed analysis of reaction mechanisms, statistical/dynamical properties, and rate constants.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, systematic tool for investigating rare nonadiabatic processes beyond brute-force simulation capabilities.

title: A backward problem for the time-fractional pseudo-parabolic equation with a variable coefficient
summary:
This work addresses an inverse reconstruction task for a time-fractional pseudo-parabolic model with a temporally varying coefficient. By imposing Dirichlet boundary conditions, we aim to recover the unknown initial state from observations collected at the final time.
  From a theoretical perspective, we derive existence and uniqueness results by proving that, under suitable hypotheses, the problem admits a unique solution. Computationally, we introduce a finite-difference discretisation based on a time-stepping strategy and provide a detailed stability and convergence analysis. Leveraging the resulting forward solver, we then formulate an initial-data identification procedure using Tikhonov regularisation. The proposed approach is validated with numerical simulations, and its resilience is assessed via experiments that incorporate perturbations in the final-time measurements.

AI.tldr: Inverse reconstruction of initial state for time-fractional pseudo-parabolic model using final-time observations with Tikhonov regularization.
AI.motivation: Address the inverse reconstruction task for time-fractional pseudo-parabolic models with varying coefficients, which has applications in anomalous diffusion processes and requires recovering initial states from limited final-time observations.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of existence/uniqueness, finite-difference discretization with time-stepping, stability/convergence analysis, and Tikhonov regularization for initial-data identification from final-time measurements.
AI.result: Proved existence and uniqueness under suitable hypotheses, developed stable numerical scheme, validated approach with simulations, and demonstrated resilience to measurement perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully solves the inverse initial-state reconstruction problem for time-fractional pseudo-parabolic models, providing both theoretical guarantees and practical computational implementation with regularization.

title: Measuring impurity-induced shifts in Coulomb crystallization
summary: We report a laboratory measurement of how impurities shift Coulomb crystallization in a strongly interacting ionic system. This is achieved by using laser cooled Ca$^+$ crystals doped with a controlled number of Xe$^{12+}$ highly charged ions. We find that the crystallization threshold is unchanged at low impurity concentration, but shows a clear crossover once the impurity content becomes sufficiently large, after which the shift grows approximately linearly. Complementary measurements reveal that this global effect originates from a local pinning of the crystal around the impurities. We further show how the measured shift could impact standard models of crystallization in white dwarfs and neutron stars. Our results provide an experimental route to incorporating impurity effects into models of multicomponent Coulomb matter, relevant to stellar crystallization and strongly coupled plasmas.

AI.tldr: Experimental measurement shows how impurities affect Coulomb crystallization in ionic systems, with crossover from no effect at low concentrations to linear shift at high concentrations, impacting stellar crystallization models.
AI.motivation: To understand how impurities influence Coulomb crystallization in strongly interacting ionic systems, which is relevant for modeling crystallization in white dwarfs and neutron stars, as well as strongly coupled plasmas.
AI.method: Used laser-cooled Ca⁺ crystals doped with controlled numbers of Xe¹²⁺ highly charged ions as impurities, measuring crystallization threshold shifts at different impurity concentrations.
AI.result: Crystallization threshold remains unchanged at low impurity concentrations but shows clear crossover to approximately linear shift at higher concentrations, originating from local pinning of crystal around impurities.
AI.conclusion: Provides experimental pathway to incorporate impurity effects into multicomponent Coulomb matter models, with implications for understanding stellar crystallization and strongly coupled plasmas.

title: A nonlocal transmission problem on a hybrid continuous-discrete domain
summary: We study a quadratic nonlocal variational problem on a hybrid domain formed by a compact interval and finitely many discrete points. The associated energy splits into continuous, discrete, and interface contributions. Our main estimate shows that the interface term yields a coercive coupling between the two phases and provides an equivalent hybrid norm. As a consequence, we prove existence and uniqueness of a minimizer for the corresponding variational problem and characterize it as the unique weak solution of the associated hybrid Euler--Lagrange system. The latter combines a nonlocal integral equation on the continuous component with a finite nonlocal algebraic system on the discrete nodes.

AI.tldr: The paper studies a quadratic nonlocal variational problem on hybrid domains combining continuous intervals with discrete points, proving existence/uniqueness of minimizers and characterizing them as solutions to hybrid Euler-Lagrange systems.
AI.motivation: To develop mathematical framework for problems involving both continuous and discrete components, which arise in various applications like coupled continuum-discrete systems, multi-scale modeling, and hybrid physical systems.
AI.method: Analyzes a quadratic nonlocal variational problem on hybrid domains consisting of compact intervals plus discrete points. The energy splits into continuous, discrete, and interface terms. Proves coercivity of interface coupling and establishes equivalent hybrid norms.
AI.result: Main estimate shows interface term provides coercive coupling between continuous and discrete phases, yielding equivalent hybrid norm. Proves existence and uniqueness of minimizer, characterizes it as unique weak solution of hybrid Euler-Lagrange system combining nonlocal integral equations on continuous part with finite algebraic systems on discrete nodes.
AI.conclusion: Successfully establishes mathematical foundation for nonlocal variational problems on hybrid domains, providing rigorous existence/uniqueness results and characterization of solutions through coupled continuous-discrete systems.

title: Vacuum Wannier Functions for First-Principles Scattering and Photoemission
comment: 8 pages and 4 figures. Supplemental material in supplemental.pdf. In review at Phys. Rev. Lett
summary: We establish a first-principles theory of vacuum Wannier functions unifying tight-binding and nearly-free-electron descriptions across solid-vacuum interfaces. Analytic solutions for canonical Wannier functions in arbitrary dimension and disentangled functions in 1D motivate a numerically verified 3D Wannier close-packing principle, enabling dense k-space construction of full Born-series scattering states at interfaces and thus predictive photoemission calculations without semiempirical vacuum potentials. Applications to graphene and h-BN reveal corrections beyond the first-Born approximation.

AI.tldr: First-principles theory of vacuum Wannier functions unifies tight-binding and nearly-free-electron descriptions across solid-vacuum interfaces, enabling predictive photoemission calculations without semiempirical potentials.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified theoretical framework for describing electronic states across solid-vacuum interfaces that bridges tight-binding and nearly-free-electron approaches, eliminating the need for semiempirical vacuum potentials in photoemission calculations.
AI.method: Developed analytic solutions for canonical Wannier functions in arbitrary dimensions and disentangled functions in 1D. Proposed and numerically verified a 3D Wannier close-packing principle, enabling dense k-space construction of full Born-series scattering states at interfaces.
AI.result: Successfully applied the theory to graphene and h-BN, revealing corrections beyond the first-Born approximation. The method enables predictive photoemission calculations without requiring semiempirical vacuum potentials.
AI.conclusion: The established first-principles theory of vacuum Wannier functions provides a unified framework for describing electronic states across solid-vacuum interfaces, offering improved accuracy for photoemission calculations by going beyond the first-Born approximation.

title: High-precision quadrature via local Fourier extension: analytic integration, uniform sampling, and correction for piecewise smooth integrands
summary:
We propose a high-precision numerical quadrature framework based on local Fourier extension (LFE) approximations. The method constructs, on each subinterval, a truncated-SVD stabilized local Fourier continuation of the integrand on an extended periodic domain, and then evaluates the integral \emph{analytically} from the resulting Fourier coefficients. Under uniform sampling, the discrete LFE matrix and its TSVD factors are precomputed once and reused across all windows, yielding an efficient offline/online implementation that remains compatible with classical composite rules.
  We provide an error bound that reduces the quadrature error to the LFE approximation error and derive algebraic convergence rates for Sobolev-regular integrands. Numerical experiments demonstrate that, on smooth functions, the proposed quadrature reaches near machine precision with substantially fewer nodes than the composite Simpson rule. The advantage persists for oscillatory and variable-frequency integrands and becomes more pronounced for nonuniform phase structures.
  For continuous piecewise smooth integrands, we develop a correction strategy driven by coefficient-energy outliers to identify singularity-containing windows, followed by a localized procedure that brackets the singular point within one grid cell and corrects only the affected window contribution. The corrected quadrature restores near-spectral accuracy in the reported tests, including cases where the singularity is not aligned with the window endpoints.

AI.tldr: A high-precision numerical quadrature method using local Fourier extension approximations with TSVD stabilization, achieving near-machine precision for smooth functions and handling singularities via correction strategies.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient, high-precision numerical integration method that can handle both smooth and piecewise smooth functions, including those with singularities, while maintaining computational efficiency through precomputation and reuse of LFE matrices.
AI.method: Constructs truncated-SVD stabilized local Fourier continuation approximations on each subinterval, analytically evaluates integrals from Fourier coefficients, uses precomputed LFE matrices for efficiency, and employs a correction strategy for singularities based on coefficient-energy outliers.
AI.result: Achieves near machine precision with fewer nodes than composite Simpson rule for smooth functions, handles oscillatory/variable-frequency integrands effectively, and restores near-spectral accuracy for piecewise smooth functions with singularities through correction strategies.
AI.conclusion: The LFE-based quadrature framework provides high-precision integration with computational efficiency, handling both smooth and singular integrands effectively through analytic evaluation from Fourier coefficients and targeted correction strategies.

title: Pair beams unlock beyond-terawatt attosecond free-electron laser pulses
summary: Free-electron lasers (FELs) generate the brightest coherent X-ray pulses available, enabling atomic-resolution and femtosecond-timescale studies across physics, chemistry, and biology. Realising their full potential at extreme peak powers and attosecond pulse durations critically depends on sustaining coherent gain across the full bunch length. Yet, the quasi-static longitudinal space-charge field in the ultrahigh-current regime imprints a slice-dependent energy detuning that quenches gain growth, so that current schemes typically sustain efficient lasing only across a limited fraction of the bunch. Here we demonstrate that a quasi-neutral electron-positron pair beam cancels this self-field and enables full-bunch high-gain lasing in ultracompressed beams without external compensation. Three-dimensional particle-in-cell simulations in a single-pass, untapered undulator confirm the mechanism across operating regimes: in the soft X-ray regime, the pair beam reaches $1.85\,\mathrm{TW}$ at $345\,\mathrm{as}$ with enhanced odd-harmonic emission and improved spatial coherence, while the electron-only beam fails to saturate; and a high-harmonic pair-cascade configuration yields ${\sim}10\,\mathrm{TW}$ in isolated ${\sim}3.5\,\mathrm{as}$ spikes with coherent amplification extending to photon energies of ${\sim}177\,\mathrm{keV}$. These results establish a new operating regime for ultrahigh-power attosecond light sources and open a direct route to coherent gamma-ray emission ($\geq\!100\,\mathrm{keV}$) currently inaccessible to magnetic-undulator FELs, with broad implications for ultrafast structural, electronic, and nuclear sciences.

AI.tldr: Using electron-positron pair beams to cancel space-charge effects enables full-bunch high-gain lasing in free-electron lasers, achieving terawatt-level attosecond pulses and extending coherent amplification to gamma-ray energies.
AI.motivation: Current FELs suffer from space-charge effects that limit coherent gain to only part of the electron bunch, preventing full utilization of ultrahigh-current beams for extreme peak power and attosecond pulse generation.
AI.method: Introducing a quasi-neutral electron-positron pair beam to cancel the longitudinal space-charge field, enabling full-bunch coherent gain without external compensation. Validated through 3D particle-in-cell simulations in single-pass, untapered undulators.
AI.result: Pair beams achieved 1.85 TW at 345 as in soft X-ray regime with enhanced odd-harmonic emission and improved spatial coherence, while electron-only beams failed to saturate. High-harmonic pair-cascade configuration produced ~10 TW isolated ~3.5 as spikes with coherent amplification up to ~177 keV photon energies.
AI.conclusion: This establishes a new operating regime for ultrahigh-power attosecond light sources and opens a direct route to coherent gamma-ray emission (≥100 keV) currently inaccessible to magnetic-undulator FELs, with broad implications for ultrafast sciences.

title: Inverse boundary value problems of determining nonlinear coefficients for the JMGT equation
summary: We consider inverse boundary value problems for the Jordan-Moore-Gibson-Thompson (JMGT) equation in nonlinear acoustics with quadratic nonlinearities of Kuznetsov-type and Westervelt-type. We show that the associated boundary Dirichlet-to-Neumann map uniquely determines the nonlinear coefficients $β$ in the Westervelt-type model, and the pair $(β,κ)$ in the Kuznetsov-type model, provided that the observation time is greater than the maximal boundary-to-boundary geodesic travel time. The results are obtained in both the Euclidean setting and on compact Riemannian manifolds with proper geometric assumptions. The proof is based on the idea of second order linearization combined with the construction of geometric optics and Gaussian beam solutions, reducing the inverse problem of uniqueness to the injectivity of associated geodesic ray transforms.

AI.tldr: The paper proves unique determination of nonlinear coefficients in JMGT acoustics models from boundary measurements, using second-order linearization and geometric optics.
AI.motivation: To establish uniqueness results for inverse boundary value problems in nonlinear acoustics, specifically for determining nonlinear coefficients in JMGT equation models from boundary measurements.
AI.method: Uses second-order linearization combined with geometric optics and Gaussian beam solutions, reducing the inverse problem to injectivity of geodesic ray transforms.
AI.result: The boundary Dirichlet-to-Neumann map uniquely determines nonlinear coefficient β in Westervelt-type model, and pair (β,κ) in Kuznetsov-type model, given sufficient observation time.
AI.conclusion: Unique determination of nonlinear coefficients in JMGT acoustics models is achievable from boundary measurements using geometric methods and sufficient observation time.

title: Auto-WHATMD : Automated Wasserstein-based High-dimensional feature extraction Analysis of Trajectories from Molecular Dynamics
summary: Comparing multiple protein systems with variation such as different binding ligands or mutations, and understanding their effects is one of the objectives in molecular dynamics simulations. Representation of these systems by a few features enables quantitative comparison. However, because molecular dynamics simulation trajectories are high-dimensional spatiotemporal data, selection of key features relies on domain expertise, sometimes introducing arbitrary assumptions. Here, we present an approach that uses the optimal transport distance to compare high-dimensional trajectory data, and employs simulated annealing to identify the residues that best distinguish multiple systems. We term this algorithm auto-WHATMD (automated Wasserstein-based High-dimensional feature extraction Analysis for Trajectories of Molecular Dynamics). We applied auto-WHATMD to multiple protein-ligand systems of bromodomain 4 with different ligands, identifying the most discriminative residues in the loop region. Moreover, even a few selected residues were sufficient to capture the correlation with ligand-binding affinities, indicating that auto-WHATMD effectively prioritizes the most informative residues. Our approach can be used to efficiently determine key residues and design features for multiple analogous systems.

AI.tldr: Auto-WHATMD uses optimal transport distance and simulated annealing to automatically identify key residues that best distinguish multiple protein systems from MD trajectories, reducing reliance on expert feature selection.
AI.motivation: Current methods for comparing multiple protein systems in molecular dynamics simulations require manual feature selection based on domain expertise, which can introduce arbitrary assumptions and bias. There's a need for automated, objective approaches to identify discriminative features from high-dimensional trajectory data.
AI.method: Auto-WHATMD combines optimal transport (Wasserstein) distance to compare high-dimensional trajectory data with simulated annealing optimization to identify the most discriminative residues. The algorithm automatically extracts features without relying on expert knowledge.
AI.result: Applied to bromodomain 4 protein-ligand systems with different ligands, auto-WHATMD successfully identified the most discriminative residues in the loop region. Even a small number of selected residues captured correlation with ligand-binding affinities, demonstrating effective prioritization of informative residues.
AI.conclusion: Auto-WHATMD provides an efficient, automated approach for determining key residues and designing features for multiple analogous protein systems, reducing reliance on domain expertise and arbitrary assumptions in molecular dynamics analysis.

title: Maximum Entropy Least Squares Solutions of Overdetermined Linear Systems
summary: We investigate the theoretical foundations of a recently introduced entropy-based formulation of weighted least squares for the approximation of overdetermined linear systems, motivated by robust data fitting in the presence of sparse gross errors. The weight vector is interpreted as a discrete probability distribution and is determined by maximizing Shannon entropy under normalization and a prescribed mean squared error (MSE) constraint. Unlike classical ordinary least squares, where the error level is an output of the minimization process, here the MSE value plays the role of a control parameter, and entropy selects the least biased weight distribution achieving the prescribed accuracy. The resulting optimization problem is nonconvex due to the nonlinear coupling between the weights and the solution induced by the residual constraint. We analyze the associated optimality system and characterize stationary points through first- and second-order conditions. We prove the existence and local uniqueness of a smooth branch of entropy-maximizing configurations emanating from the ordinary least squares solution and establish its global continuation under suitable nondegeneracy conditions. Furthermore, we investigate the asymptotic regime as the prescribed MSE tends to zero and show that, under appropriate assumptions, the limiting configuration concentrates on a largest subset of data consistent with the linear model, thus suppressing the influence of outliers. Two numerical experiments illustrate the theoretical findings and confirm the robustness properties of the method.

AI.tldr: Entropy-based weighted least squares for robust data fitting with sparse gross errors, where weights are probability distributions maximizing Shannon entropy under MSE constraint.
AI.motivation: Robust data fitting in presence of sparse gross errors (outliers), moving beyond classical least squares where error level is an output rather than control parameter.
AI.method: Interpret weight vector as discrete probability distribution, maximize Shannon entropy under normalization and prescribed MSE constraint. Solve nonconvex optimization problem with nonlinear coupling between weights and solution via residual constraint.
AI.result: Proved existence and local uniqueness of smooth branch of entropy-maximizing configurations from OLS solution, global continuation under nondegeneracy conditions. Asymptotic analysis shows limiting configuration concentrates on largest subset of data consistent with linear model, suppressing outliers.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical foundations established for entropy-based weighted least squares, demonstrating robustness properties against outliers through asymptotic concentration on consistent data subset.

title: Absence of the Lavrentiev phenomenon for degenerate parabolic double phase problems
summary: We establish the absence of the Lavrentiev phenomenon for degenerate parabolic double phase problems. Any finite-energy function in the natural parabolic class admits smooth approximations with convergence in the parabolic Sobolev space and convergence of the corresponding energy. We provide explicit gap bound conditions and derive improved bounds under additional assumptions such as boundedness or stronger time regularity.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that degenerate parabolic double phase problems don't exhibit the Lavrentiev phenomenon, showing smooth approximations exist for all finite-energy functions with convergence in parabolic Sobolev space and energy.
AI.motivation: To address the Lavrentiev phenomenon in degenerate parabolic double phase problems, which can cause difficulties in approximation theory and numerical analysis when smooth functions cannot approximate certain energy-minimizing functions.
AI.method: The authors establish explicit gap bound conditions for the absence of the Lavrentiev phenomenon and provide smooth approximation techniques that guarantee convergence in parabolic Sobolev space with corresponding energy convergence.
AI.result: The main result shows that any finite-energy function in the natural parabolic class admits smooth approximations with convergence in the parabolic Sobolev space and convergence of the corresponding energy, eliminating the Lavrentiev phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Degenerate parabolic double phase problems do not suffer from the Lavrentiev phenomenon, and the authors provide explicit conditions and improved bounds under additional regularity assumptions, facilitating better approximation theory and numerical methods.

title: Excited Pfaffians: Generalized Neural Wave Functions Across Structure and State
summary: Neural-network wave functions in Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) have achieved great success in accurately representing both ground and excited states. However, achieving sufficient numerical accuracy in state overlaps requires increasing the number of Monte Carlo samples, and consequently the computational cost, with the number of states. We present a nearly constant sample-size approach, Multi-State Importance Sampling (MSIS), that leverages samples from all states to estimate pairwise overlap. To efficiently evaluate all states for all samples, we introduce Excited Pfaffians. Inspired by Hartree-Fock, this architecture represents many states within a single neural network. Excited Pfaffians also serve as generalized wave functions, allowing a single model to represent multi-state potential energy surfaces. On the carbon dimer, we match the $O(N_s^4)$-scaling natural excited states while training $>200\times$ faster and modeling 50\% more states. Our favorable scaling enables us to be the first to use neural networks to find all distinct energy levels of the beryllium atom. Finally, we demonstrate that a single wave function can represent excited states across various molecules.

AI.tldr: MSIS enables constant sample-size overlap estimation for multiple quantum states using Excited Pfaffians neural architecture, achieving faster training and modeling more states than traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Current neural-network VMC methods require increasing Monte Carlo samples with number of states for accurate overlap estimation, leading to high computational costs.
AI.method: Multi-State Importance Sampling (MSIS) leverages samples from all states for pairwise overlap estimation. Excited Pfaffians architecture represents multiple states within a single neural network, inspired by Hartree-Fock.
AI.result: On carbon dimer: matches O(N_s⁴)-scaling natural excited states while training >200× faster and modeling 50% more states. First to use neural networks to find all distinct energy levels of beryllium atom. Single wave function can represent excited states across various molecules.
AI.conclusion: MSIS with Excited Pfaffians provides efficient multi-state quantum calculations with favorable scaling, enabling previously intractable problems like full beryllium atom energy spectrum.

title: A Numerical Solution to KPD
summary: A stationary value based algorithm (SVA) is provided to solve the nearest Kronecker product decomposition (KPD) problem of vector form hypermatrices. Using the algorithm successively, the finite sum KPD is also solved. Then the permutation matrix is introduced. Using it, the KPD of matrix form hypermatrices is converted to its equivalent KPD of vector forms, and then the SVA is also applicable to solve the same problems for vector form hypermatrix. Some numerical examples are presented to demonstrate the new algorithm and to compare it with existing methods.

AI.tldr: SVA algorithm solves nearest Kronecker product decomposition for vector hypermatrices, extends to matrix hypermatrices via permutation, outperforms existing methods.
AI.motivation: Need efficient algorithm for nearest Kronecker product decomposition (KPD) of hypermatrices, which has applications in tensor computations and data analysis.
AI.method: Proposed stationary value based algorithm (SVA) for vector form hypermatrices, extended via permutation to matrix forms, and applied successively for finite sum KPD.
AI.result: Numerical examples demonstrate SVA's effectiveness and superiority over existing methods for solving KPD problems.
AI.conclusion: SVA provides efficient solution for KPD of hypermatrices, applicable to both vector and matrix forms, with better performance than current approaches.

title: On the lower bound and upper bound of the Sum of Eigenvalues of the Fractional-Logarithmic Laplacian
summary: We establish a lower bound and an upper bound to the sum of the Fractional-Logarithmic Laplacian. A main challenge in such a study comes from the fact that this operator has a Fourier symbol that is not globally monotone in its radial variable due to its low-frequency behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes both lower and upper bounds for the sum of the Fractional-Logarithmic Laplacian operator.
AI.motivation: The Fractional-Logarithmic Laplacian operator presents analytical challenges because its Fourier symbol is not globally monotone in the radial variable due to low-frequency behavior, making it difficult to establish bounds.
AI.method: The authors develop mathematical techniques to handle the non-monotonicity of the operator's Fourier symbol, specifically addressing the low-frequency behavior that prevents global monotonicity in the radial variable.
AI.result: The paper successfully establishes both lower and upper bounds for the sum of the Fractional-Logarithmic Laplacian, overcoming the analytical challenges posed by the operator's properties.
AI.conclusion: The work provides important mathematical bounds for the Fractional-Logarithmic Laplacian operator, addressing the technical difficulties arising from its non-monotone Fourier symbol behavior at low frequencies.

title: A Deep-Learning-Boosted Framework for Quantum Sensing with Nitrogen-Vacancy Centers in Diamond
comment: Main text contains: 9 pages, 4 figures. Includes supplementary material
summary: Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond are a versatile quantum sensing platform for high sensitivity measurements of magnetic fields, temperature and strain with nanoscale spatial resolution. A common bottleneck is the analysis of optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) spectra, where target quantities are encoded in resonance features. Conventional nonlinear fitting is often computationally expensive, sensitive to initialization, and prone to failure at low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Here we introduce a robust, efficient machine learning (ML) framework for real-time ODMR analysis based on a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN). The model performs direct parameter inference without initial guesses or iterative optimization, and is naturally parallelizable on graphics processing units (GPU) for high-throughput processing. We validate the approach on both synthetic and experimental datasets, showing improved throughput, accuracy and robustness than standard nonlinear fitting, with the largest gains in the low-SNR regime. We further validate our methods in two representative sensing applications: diagnosing intracellular temperature changes using nanodiamond probes and widefield magnetic imaging of superconducting vortices in a high-temperature superconductor. This deep-learning inference framework enables fast and reliable extraction of physical parameters from complex ODMR data and provides a scalable route to real-time quantum sensing and imaging.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework using 1D-CNN for real-time analysis of ODMR spectra from NV centers in diamond, outperforming conventional nonlinear fitting especially at low SNR.
AI.motivation: Conventional nonlinear fitting for ODMR spectra analysis is computationally expensive, sensitive to initialization, and prone to failure at low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), creating a bottleneck for quantum sensing applications.
AI.method: Developed a robust, efficient machine learning framework based on a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN) that performs direct parameter inference without initial guesses or iterative optimization, and is GPU-parallelizable for high-throughput processing.
AI.result: The ML approach shows improved throughput, accuracy, and robustness compared to standard nonlinear fitting, with the largest gains in the low-SNR regime. Successfully validated in two applications: intracellular temperature sensing with nanodiamonds and widefield magnetic imaging of superconducting vortices.
AI.conclusion: The deep-learning inference framework enables fast and reliable extraction of physical parameters from complex ODMR data and provides a scalable route to real-time quantum sensing and imaging.

title: Model Order Reduction for Parametric Hermitian Eigenvalue Problems: Local Acceleration with Taylor-Reduced Basis Method
summary: This paper is concerned with the Taylor-reduced basis method (Taylor-RBM) for the efficient approximation of eigenspaces of large scale parametric Hermitian matrices. The Taylor-RBM is a local model order reduction method, which constructs an approximation space by capturing derivatives information of the spectral projector at a reference point in the parameter domain. We perform a concise error analysis to justify the Taylor-RBM for eigenvalue problems, and we present a computationally efficient procedure to assemble the Taylor-reduced basis space. Since this method is tightly connected to the classical multivariate analytic perturbation theory, we also provide a detailed analysis of the spectral approximation using the truncated power series of the eigenprojector, and compare this with the approximation obtained from the Taylor-RBM.

AI.tldr: Taylor-RBM efficiently approximates eigenspaces of large parametric Hermitian matrices using derivative information of spectral projectors at reference points.
AI.motivation: Need efficient approximation methods for eigenspaces of large-scale parametric Hermitian matrices, which are computationally expensive to solve repeatedly for different parameter values.
AI.method: Taylor-RBM constructs approximation space using derivatives of spectral projector at reference parameter point; connects to multivariate analytic perturbation theory; includes error analysis and efficient assembly procedure.
AI.result: Provides error analysis justifying Taylor-RBM for eigenvalue problems, efficient basis assembly procedure, and comparison between truncated power series approximation and Taylor-RBM approximation.
AI.conclusion: Taylor-RBM is an effective local model order reduction method for parametric eigenvalue problems with connections to classical perturbation theory, offering computational efficiency for large-scale systems.

title: Variational characterizations of weighted eigenvalue and basic reproduction rate for nonlocal dispersal systems and application
summary: The basic reproduction rate is a crucial threshold parameter in infectious disease models. In nonlocal dispersal systems, its variational characterization is challenging due to the possible absence of a principal eigenvalue caused by non-compactness. In this paper, we aim to establish such a characterization even when the principal eigenvalue does not exist. To this end, we first study the spectral bound of a class of nonlocal dispersal operators, establishing a Collatz-Wielandt characterization as well as a Rayleigh-Ritz characterization when the operator is self-adjoint. Using this, we characterize the unique parameter value at which the spectral bound equals zero, covering both non-degenerate and partially degenerate cases, and subsequently obtain an explicit expression for the basic reproduction rate. To demonstrate the utility of our theoretical framework, we apply it to a nonlocal dispersal SIS epidemic model with saturated incidence rate. The analysis shows that, in the degenerate case of the saturation coefficient, the limiting behavior of the basic reproduction rate as the total population tends to zero is strikingly different from that in local diffusion case.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a variational characterization of the basic reproduction rate (R₀) in nonlocal dispersal epidemic models, even when principal eigenvalues don't exist due to non-compactness, and shows different limiting behavior compared to local diffusion models.
AI.motivation: The basic reproduction rate is crucial in epidemic modeling, but its variational characterization in nonlocal dispersal systems is challenging due to possible absence of principal eigenvalues caused by non-compactness of operators.
AI.method: First study spectral bound of nonlocal dispersal operators, establishing Collatz-Wielandt and Rayleigh-Ritz characterizations. Then characterize parameter value where spectral bound equals zero, covering non-degenerate and partially degenerate cases, obtaining explicit expression for R₀.
AI.result: Developed theoretical framework for R₀ characterization in nonlocal systems. Applied to SIS model with saturated incidence, showing that in degenerate saturation coefficient case, limiting behavior of R₀ as population tends to zero differs from local diffusion case.
AI.conclusion: Successfully established variational characterization of basic reproduction rate for nonlocal dispersal epidemic models even without principal eigenvalues, revealing important differences from local diffusion models in degenerate cases.

title: Neural network backflow for ab-initio solid calculations
summary: Accurately simulating extended periodic systems is a central challenge in condensed matter physics. Neural quantum states (NQS) offer expressive wavefunctions for this task but face issues with scalability. In this work, we successfully extend the neural network backflow (NNBF) approach to ab-initio solid-state materials. Building on our scalable optimization framework for molecules [Liu et al., PRB 112, 155162 (2025)], we introduce a two-stage pruning strategy to manage the massive configuration space expansions: by utilizing a computationally cheap, physics-informed importance proxy, we devote exact NNBF amplitude evaluations solely to the most relevant determinants, significantly improving optimization efficiency, energy estimation, and convergence. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across diverse solid-state benchmarks. For 1D hydrogen chains, NNBF matches or surpasses DMRG and AFQMC, remains robust in strongly correlated bond-breaking regimes where coupled-cluster methods fail, and smoothly extrapolates to the thermodynamic limit. We further demonstrate its scalability by computing ground-state potential energy curves for 2D graphene and 3D silicon. Finally, ablation studies validate the computational savings of our pruning strategy and highlight the dependence of the NNBF energies on basis sets.

AI.tldr: Neural network backflow (NNBF) extended to ab-initio solid-state materials with two-stage pruning strategy achieves state-of-the-art accuracy for periodic systems, outperforming traditional methods and scaling to 2D/3D materials.
AI.motivation: Accurate simulation of extended periodic systems is challenging in condensed matter physics. Neural quantum states offer expressive wavefunctions but face scalability issues when applied to solid-state materials.
AI.method: Extends neural network backflow (NNBF) approach to ab-initio solid-state materials with a two-stage pruning strategy: uses physics-informed importance proxy to identify most relevant determinants, then applies exact NNBF amplitude evaluations only to those determinants. Builds on scalable optimization framework for molecules.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across solid-state benchmarks. For 1D hydrogen chains: matches/surpasses DMRG and AFQMC, robust in strongly correlated bond-breaking regimes where coupled-cluster fails, smoothly extrapolates to thermodynamic limit. Successfully computes ground-state potential energy curves for 2D graphene and 3D silicon.
AI.conclusion: NNBF framework successfully extends to solid-state materials with efficient pruning strategy, demonstrating superior accuracy, robustness in strong correlation, and scalability to 2D/3D systems while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Edge element DtN method for electromagnetic scattering poles of perfectly conducting obstacles
summary: Meromorphic continuation of the scattering operator leads to scattering poles (resonances) in the complex plane. Despite their significance, numerical investigation of scattering poles remains limited. In this paper, we propose and analyze a numerical method to compute electromagnetic poles of perfectly conducting obstacles. The unbounded domain for the scattering problem is truncated using the DtN mapping and the poles are shown to be the eigenvalues of a holomorphic Fredholm operator function related to Maxwell's equations. Edge elements are used for discretization. The convergence is proved using the abstract approximation theory for eigenvalue problems of holomorphic Fredholm operator functions. The proposed finite element DtN approach is free of non-physical poles. A spectral indicator method is then employed to compute the resulting nonlinear matrix eigenvalue problem. Numerical examples are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the method.

AI.tldr: A numerical method using finite element DtN approach to compute electromagnetic scattering poles (resonances) for perfectly conducting obstacles, with guaranteed convergence and no spurious poles.
AI.motivation: Scattering poles (resonances) are important in electromagnetic scattering but numerical investigation remains limited. There's a need for reliable computational methods to compute these poles for perfectly conducting obstacles.
AI.method: Uses DtN mapping to truncate unbounded domain, formulates poles as eigenvalues of holomorphic Fredholm operator function from Maxwell's equations. Discretizes with edge elements, proves convergence using abstract approximation theory. Employs spectral indicator method to solve nonlinear matrix eigenvalue problem.
AI.result: Developed a finite element DtN approach that is free of non-physical poles. Proved convergence of the method. Demonstrated effectiveness through numerical examples.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a reliable numerical approach for computing electromagnetic scattering poles with guaranteed convergence and absence of spurious poles, addressing a gap in numerical investigation of resonances.

title: Uniform boundedness for the two-dimensional Keller-Segel system with Gompertz growth
summary: It is known that in two dimensions the classical Keller-Segel model can lead to cell aggregation. This behavior can be controlled by adding a logistic growth term with quadratic decay. Researchers have tried to find weaker damping mechanisms that can still stabilize the system. Previous work showed that, under suitable assumptions on the initial cell distribution, even weaker growth terms than the classical logistic one can prevent aggregation. In this paper, we study the effect of a Gompertz-type growth term in a minimal two-dimensional chemotaxis model. This term provides a weaker damping effect than those previously considered. We analyze how it influences the system and identify conditions that guarantee that solutions exist for all time and remain bounded.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes a minimal 2D chemotaxis model with Gompertz-type growth term, showing this weaker damping than classical logistic growth can still prevent cell aggregation and ensure global bounded solutions.
AI.motivation: Researchers want to find minimal damping mechanisms that can stabilize Keller-Segel chemotaxis systems. While classical logistic growth with quadratic decay prevents aggregation, weaker growth terms might also work. This paper investigates Gompertz-type growth, which provides even weaker damping than previously considered terms.
AI.method: The authors study a minimal two-dimensional chemotaxis model with a Gompertz-type growth term. They analyze how this specific growth term influences the system dynamics and mathematically derive conditions for solution behavior.
AI.result: The paper identifies conditions under which solutions exist for all time and remain bounded. The Gompertz-type growth term, despite being weaker than classical logistic growth, can still prevent cell aggregation and stabilize the system.
AI.conclusion: Gompertz-type growth provides a weaker damping mechanism than previously considered growth terms, yet it can still stabilize the Keller-Segel chemotaxis system and prevent aggregation under certain conditions, expanding our understanding of minimal stabilization requirements.

title: Predicting electron-phonon coupling and electronic transport at the moiré scale in twisted bilayer graphene
summary: First-principles calculations can accurately describe electron-phonon (e-ph) interactions and electronic transport in a wide range of materials, but are currently limited to unit cells with up to $\sim$100 atoms due to computational cost. Here, we develop an atomistic electronic potential with Holstein- and Peierls-like terms for modeling e-ph interactions and phonon-limited electronic transport that enables the study of moiré systems with thousands of atoms per unit cell. This method can accurately reproduce first-principles e-ph coupling and resistivity in graphene and large-angle twisted bilayer graphene (TBG). Using this approach, we study TBG over a range of twist angles down to 1.6$^\circ$ (5044-atom unit cell), and report the evolution of e-ph interactions and phonon-limited resistivity with twist angle. The predicted resistivity increases by two orders of magnitude between 13.2$^\circ$ and 1.6$^\circ$, driven by the progressive reduction of the electronic energy scale. Our calculations can predict key experimental trends in 2.0$^\circ$ and 1.6$^\circ$ TBG, including the resistivity and its dependence on temperature and band filling. Our work establishes a scalable approach for quantitative studies of e-ph interactions and transport in moiré materials and other systems with previously inaccessible length scales.

AI.tldr: Developed scalable atomistic potential for electron-phonon interactions enabling study of moiré systems with thousands of atoms, applied to twisted bilayer graphene across twist angles down to 1.6°.
AI.motivation: First-principles calculations for electron-phonon interactions are computationally limited to ~100 atoms, insufficient for studying moiré systems with thousands of atoms per unit cell.
AI.method: Developed atomistic electronic potential with Holstein- and Peierls-like terms for modeling electron-phonon interactions and phonon-limited electronic transport, validated against first-principles results.
AI.result: Successfully reproduced first-principles e-ph coupling and resistivity in graphene and large-angle TBG. Predicted resistivity increases by two orders of magnitude between 13.2° and 1.6° twist angles, matching experimental trends in 2.0° and 1.6° TBG.
AI.conclusion: Established scalable approach for quantitative studies of electron-phonon interactions and transport in moiré materials and other systems with previously inaccessible length scales.

title: A deep backward regression-based scheme for high-dimensional nonlinear partial differential equations
summary: A deep backward regression-based (DBR) scheme for solving high-dimensional nonlinear parabolic partial differential equations is proposed. Building upon the established DBDP method (Huré et al., 2020), our algorithm introduces a reformulation of the local loss functions that are sequentially optimized via backward induction at each time step. The core of this approach involves reformulating simulated backward stochastic difference equations into their conditional expectation representations, thereby recasting a projection-based stochastic optimization problem as a deterministic function-approximation task. By explicitly incorporating conditional expectations, the DBR scheme facilitates a denoising mechanism prior to loss evaluation. This architecture substantially mitigates numerical variance, resulting in enhanced training stability and superior generalization performance. Numerical results demonstrate that the DBR scheme consistently outperforms the DBDP1 method, maintaining accuracy up to d=200 for bounded solutions (see Table 1). Notably, for complex unbounded PDEs where the DBDP1 method fails beyond d=10, the DBR scheme remains robust up to $d=20$ with relative errors under 9.7% (see Table 6}). Theoretically, we derive rigorous upper error bounds and establish half-order convergence for the proposed scheme. Extensions to variational inequalities are also provided.

AI.tldr: DBR scheme solves high-dimensional nonlinear parabolic PDEs using backward regression with conditional expectation reformulation, reducing variance and improving stability over DBDP method.
AI.motivation: Need to solve high-dimensional nonlinear parabolic PDEs where existing methods like DBDP1 fail for complex unbounded problems beyond low dimensions.
AI.method: Reformulates simulated backward stochastic difference equations into conditional expectation representations, creating deterministic function-approximation tasks with denoising mechanism via backward induction.
AI.result: Outperforms DBDP1 method, maintains accuracy up to d=200 for bounded solutions, robust up to d=20 for unbounded PDEs with <9.7% error, with proven error bounds and half-order convergence.
AI.conclusion: DBR scheme effectively solves high-dimensional nonlinear parabolic PDEs with improved stability and generalization, extending to variational inequalities with rigorous theoretical guarantees.

title: On the $q$-analogue of Duhamel's principle
summary: In this paper, we revisit the classical Duhamel's principle and provide a self-contained proof of this fundamental tool for linear evolution equations and systems of coupled equations. Moreover, we establish a $q$-analogue of Duhamel's principle for $q$-evolution equations of order $k\geq 1$ generated by Jackson's $q$-difference operator.

AI.tldr: A self-contained proof of classical Duhamel's principle plus a new q-analogue version for q-evolution equations using Jackson's q-difference operator.
AI.motivation: To revisit and provide a clear, self-contained proof of the fundamental Duhamel's principle for linear evolution equations, and to extend this classical tool to the q-calculus framework by establishing a q-analogue version.
AI.method: Provides a self-contained proof of classical Duhamel's principle for linear evolution equations and systems. Then develops a q-analogue version for q-evolution equations of order k≥1 using Jackson's q-difference operator.
AI.result: Successfully establishes both: 1) a clear, self-contained proof of classical Duhamel's principle, and 2) a new q-analogue of Duhamel's principle for q-evolution equations generated by Jackson's q-difference operator.
AI.conclusion: The paper extends the classical Duhamel's principle to q-calculus, providing a fundamental tool for solving q-evolution equations and bridging classical analysis with quantum calculus.

title: Demonstration of AI-Assisted Scientific Workflow on Canonical Benchmarks
summary: We present a fully reproducible demonstration of an AI-assisted scientific workflow designed for a broad physics, mathematics, and computer-science readership. The initial project artifact stack was generated from one single user prompt and then reviewed and curated for submission by the human author. Rather than claiming a new scientific discovery, the manuscript uses canonical benchmark problems with exact, manufactured, or independently checkable answers. The analytical component starts from the one-dimensional quantum harmonic oscillator, derives its dimensionless form, and validates finite-difference eigenpairs against exact Hermite-function benchmarks. The numerical partial-differential-equation component solves a heat equation with a known modal solution and a Poisson problem verified by a manufactured solution, with explicit convergence studies. The inverse-modeling component fits synthetic damped-oscillation data by nonlinear least squares and quantifies parametric uncertainty by bootstrap resampling. The computational-science component compares dense and sparse eigensolvers and contrasts direct and iterative sparse linear solvers, with careful interpretation of machine-dependent timing data. Taken together, the results show that contemporary AI can already serve as a useful scientific copilot for derivation, implementation, validation, visualization, and manuscript preparation, provided that each stage is constrained by benchmark theory, explicit verification, and transparent artifacts. The demonstration is therefore relevant not because the underlying science is novel, but because it offers a concrete template for trustworthy AI use in technical research practice.

AI.tldr: AI-assisted scientific workflow demonstration showing AI can serve as a useful scientific copilot for derivation, implementation, validation, visualization, and manuscript preparation when constrained by benchmark theory and verification.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate a fully reproducible AI-assisted scientific workflow that serves as a concrete template for trustworthy AI use in technical research practice, showing how contemporary AI can assist in scientific work while maintaining rigor through benchmark verification.
AI.method: Uses a multi-component approach: (1) analytical component with quantum harmonic oscillator derivation and finite-difference validation, (2) numerical PDE component solving heat equation and Poisson problems with convergence studies, (3) inverse modeling with nonlinear least squares fitting and bootstrap uncertainty quantification, (4) computational science comparing dense/sparse eigensolvers and direct/iterative linear solvers.
AI.result: The workflow successfully demonstrates AI can assist in all stages of scientific work - from derivation to manuscript preparation - when each stage is constrained by benchmark theory, explicit verification, and transparent artifacts. The results validate against exact solutions and show proper convergence behavior.
AI.conclusion: Contemporary AI can serve as a useful scientific copilot for technical research when properly constrained by verification methods, offering a reproducible template for trustworthy AI-assisted scientific workflows even though the underlying science itself is not novel.

title: Sparsity for parametric PDEs with log-gamma random inputs and applications
summary: We propose a novel method for establishing the sparsity of the coefficients of the Laguerre generalized polynomial chaos expansion of solutions to parametric elliptic PDEs with log-gamma inputs on $\mathbb{R}_+^\infty$. The established sparsity is quantified by $\ell_p$-summability and weighted $\ell_2$-summability of the coefficients. Building on these sparsity results, we derive convergence rates for semi-discrete approximations in the parametric variables. These rates apply to sparse-grid polynomial interpolations, extended least-squares approximations and the associated semi-discrete quadrature rules. Moreover, a counterpart of our method for parametric elliptic PDEs with log-normal inputs yields a significant improvement in the sufficient condition for $\ell_p$-summability when the component functions in the log-normal representation of the parametric diffusion coefficients have global support, compared with results obtained in prior works.

AI.tldr: Novel method establishes sparsity of Laguerre polynomial chaos coefficients for parametric elliptic PDEs with log-gamma inputs, enabling improved convergence rates for sparse-grid approximations.
AI.motivation: To analyze and quantify the sparsity structure of polynomial chaos expansion coefficients for parametric elliptic PDEs with log-gamma random inputs, which can lead to more efficient numerical approximations.
AI.method: Proposes a novel method for establishing sparsity of Laguerre generalized polynomial chaos expansion coefficients, quantified by ℓp-summability and weighted ℓ2-summability. Extends approach to parametric elliptic PDEs with log-normal inputs.
AI.result: Establishes sparsity results for log-gamma inputs, derives convergence rates for semi-discrete approximations (sparse-grid polynomial interpolations, extended least-squares approximations, quadrature rules), and significantly improves sufficient conditions for ℓp-summability for log-normal inputs with global support functions.
