AI.conclusion: The method successfully quantifies coefficient sparsity for parametric elliptic PDEs with log-gamma inputs, enabling efficient numerical approximations, and provides improved theoretical results for log-normal inputs compared to prior works.

title: On the Limiting Behavior of $L^2$-Critical Pseudo-Relativistic Fermi Systems
summary: We consider ground states of a pseudo-relativistic Fermi system in the $L^2$-critical case. We prove that the system admits ground states, if and only if the attractive strength $a$ satisfies $0<a<D_{4/3,2}$, where $D_{4/3,2}\in(0, \infty)$ is the optimal constant of a dual fractional Lieb--Thirring inequality. The limiting behavior of ground states for the system is further analyzed as $a\nearrow D_{4/3,2}$. As a byproduct, the qualitative properties of optimizers for the dual fractional Lieb-Thirring inequality are also investigated.

AI.tldr: The paper proves existence of ground states for a pseudo-relativistic Fermi system in the L²-critical case, establishes a threshold condition for attractive strength, and analyzes limiting behavior of ground states.
AI.motivation: To understand ground states of pseudo-relativistic Fermi systems in the L²-critical case, which is important for studying quantum systems with relativistic effects and attractive interactions.
AI.method: Uses mathematical analysis techniques including variational methods, dual fractional Lieb-Thirring inequality, and asymptotic analysis to study ground state existence and properties.
AI.result: Proves ground states exist if and only if attractive strength a satisfies 0 < a < D_{4/3,2}, where D_{4/3,2} is the optimal constant of a dual fractional Lieb-Thirring inequality. Analyzes limiting behavior as a approaches this threshold.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a precise threshold condition for ground state existence in pseudo-relativistic Fermi systems and provides insights into the behavior of these quantum states near critical parameters.

title: Real-time probabilistic tsunami forecasting in Cascadia from sparse offshore pressure observations
summary: Near-field tsunami early warning in the Cascadia Subduction Zone is limited by sparse offshore observations. We show that a hypothetical network of 175 seafloor pressure sensors can support real-time Bayesian inference of tsunamigenic seafloor motion and probabilistic tsunami forecasts for two fully-coupled Cascadia earthquake dynamic rupture--tsunami scenarios, a partial rupture and a margin-wide rupture. The complex oceanic acoustic, Rayleigh, and tsunami wavefields in both scenarios are similar during the first two minutes and then diverge. Using an acoustic--gravity inversion with offline precomputation and online assimilation of pressure data, tsunami forecasts are obtained in less than a second. We leverage a Bayesian inversion-based framework that splits the computations into an offline precomputation phase performed with large-scale computing facilities, and an online phase that computes forecasts from real-time data and can be executed on a laptop. Forecast errors remain low at 22.1% for the margin-wide rupture and 19.6% for the partial rupture.

AI.tldr: A network of 175 seafloor pressure sensors enables real-time Bayesian tsunami forecasting for Cascadia earthquakes using acoustic-gravity inversion with offline precomputation and online data assimilation.
AI.motivation: Near-field tsunami early warning in the Cascadia Subduction Zone is limited by sparse offshore observations, creating a need for improved real-time forecasting capabilities.
AI.method: Developed a Bayesian inversion-based framework using 175 seafloor pressure sensors to detect tsunamigenic seafloor motion. Uses acoustic-gravity inversion with offline precomputation phase (large-scale computing) and online phase (real-time data assimilation on a laptop).
AI.result: Tsunami forecasts obtained in less than a second with low forecast errors: 22.1% for margin-wide rupture and 19.6% for partial rupture scenarios. The method successfully distinguishes between different rupture scenarios after initial similarity in wavefields.
AI.conclusion: A dense network of seafloor pressure sensors combined with Bayesian inversion enables rapid, accurate tsunami forecasting for Cascadia earthquakes, overcoming limitations of sparse offshore observations.

title: A well-balanced positivity-preserving discontinuous Galerkin method for shallow water models with variable density
summary: In this paper, we present a numerical scheme designed for coupled systems of variable-topography shallow water flow and solute transport. By integrating a variable-density system with an expression for relative density of mixtures, a novel formulation of the coupled system is derived. To ensure the well-balanced property, auxiliary variables are introduced to reformulate the variable-density shallow water equations into a new form, which is then discretized using the discontinuous Galerkin (DG) method with the Lax-Friedrichs (LF) flux as the numerical flux. By selecting appropriate values for the auxiliary variables, we demonstrate that the proposed method accurately preserves steady-state solutions under still water conditions, thereby verifying its well-balanced nature. Furthermore, sufficient conditions for preserving the positivity of both water depth and concentration are proposed and rigorously proven. A positivity-preserving limiter is introduced to enforce these conditions. Finally, a series of numerical examples are conducted to validate the computational accuracy and effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A well-balanced, positivity-preserving DG method for coupled shallow water flow and solute transport with variable topography
AI.motivation: To develop a robust numerical scheme for coupled variable-topography shallow water flow and solute transport systems that maintains physical properties like steady-state preservation and positivity of water depth and concentration.
AI.method: Introduces auxiliary variables to reformulate variable-density shallow water equations into a well-balanced form, then discretizes using discontinuous Galerkin method with Lax-Friedrichs flux. Includes positivity-preserving limiter for water depth and concentration.
AI.result: The method accurately preserves steady-state solutions under still water conditions (well-balanced), maintains positivity of water depth and concentration, and demonstrates computational accuracy through numerical examples.
AI.conclusion: The proposed numerical scheme successfully addresses key challenges in simulating coupled shallow water-solute transport systems by ensuring well-balanced properties and positivity preservation while maintaining computational accuracy.

title: Concentrated solutions to fractional Schrödinger-Poisson system with non-homogeneous potentials
summary: This paper mainly investigates several limit properties of normalized solutions for the fractional Schrödinger-Poisson system, including existence, concentration behaviors and local uniqueness. It is worth noting that our results on the existence and asymptotic behaviors of normalized solutions are obtained in a doubly nonlocal setting and without assuming homogeneity of the potential, which generalize the results in \cite{GDCDS} in several aspects and improve our previous work in \cite{LIUYANG}. Meanwhile, some precise properties of solution sequence such as energy estimates, decay estimates and uniform regularity are also established by introducing some new techniques.

AI.tldr: This paper studies normalized solutions for fractional Schrödinger-Poisson systems, focusing on existence, concentration behaviors, and local uniqueness in a doubly nonlocal setting without homogeneous potential assumptions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to extend previous results on fractional Schrödinger-Poisson systems by considering a more general doubly nonlocal setting without requiring homogeneous potentials, which generalizes existing literature and improves upon the authors' previous work.
AI.method: The authors investigate normalized solutions using analytical techniques to establish existence, concentration behaviors, and local uniqueness. They develop new methods to obtain precise properties including energy estimates, decay estimates, and uniform regularity for solution sequences.
AI.result: The paper obtains existence and asymptotic behaviors of normalized solutions in a doubly nonlocal setting without homogeneous potential assumptions, generalizing previous results. It also establishes precise properties like energy estimates, decay estimates, and uniform regularity through new technical approaches.
AI.conclusion: The research successfully extends the understanding of fractional Schrödinger-Poisson systems by providing comprehensive results on normalized solutions in a more general setting, with improved technical methods that advance the field beyond previous limitations.

title: Loss of altermagnetic order and smooth restoration of Kramers' spin degeneracy with increasing temperature in CrSb and MnTe
summary: We describe how thermally induced spin fluctuations modify the electronic structures of two prototypical altermagnets, CrSb and MnTe, via application of the disordered local moment picture. For both materials, our self-consistent, ab initio calculations demonstrate that local magnetic moments persist on Cr and Mn atoms in their paramagnetic states, necessitating a spin-polarised description of the electronic structure even above the Néel temperature, $T_\mathrm{N}$. Moreover, Kramers' spin degeneracy, which is broken for both materials in their altermagnetic ground states, is shown to be smoothly restored - on the average - as the local moments thermally disorder. In metallic CrSb, this occurs at temperatures well below $T_\mathrm{N}$ and the signature effects of its altermagnetism are lost as the magnetic disorder induces heavy smearing of strongly dispersive electronic states around the Fermi energy. By contrast, in semiconducting MnTe, with its band gap largely unaffected by magnetic disorder, the spin degeneracy only returns at temperatures close to and above $T_\mathrm{N}$. We quantify the temperature dependence of the altermagnetic order parameter and the underlying electronic structures of both materials, with significant implications for their spin transport properties.

AI.tldr: Thermal spin fluctuations in altermagnets CrSb and MnTe modify electronic structures, with local moments persisting above Néel temperature, requiring spin-polarized description even in paramagnetic state.
AI.motivation: To understand how thermally induced spin fluctuations affect electronic structures of altermagnets and how magnetic disorder impacts spin degeneracy and transport properties.
AI.method: Self-consistent ab initio calculations using disordered local moment picture to study CrSb and MnTe, analyzing temperature dependence of altermagnetic order parameter and electronic structures.
AI.result: Local magnetic moments persist on Cr/Mn atoms in paramagnetic states; Kramers' spin degeneracy is smoothly restored with thermal disorder; CrSb loses altermagnetic signatures well below TN due to smearing, while MnTe maintains gap until near TN.
AI.conclusion: Thermal spin fluctuations significantly modify altermagnetic electronic structures, with different behaviors in metallic vs semiconducting materials, impacting spin transport properties.

title: Well-posedness and instability of free electron quantum tomography
summary: Recent advancements in photon induced near-field electron microscopy (PINEM) enable the preparation, coherent manipulation and characterization of free-electron quantum states. The available measurement consists of electron energy spectrograms and the goal is the reconstruction of a density matrix representing the quantum state. This requires the solution of a constrained linear inverse problem, where a positive semi-definite trace-class operator is reconstructed given its diagonal in different bases. We show the well-posedness of this problem by exploiting the regularizing effect of the positive semi-definiteness constraint. Unusually, well-posedness in this case does not imply any stability estimates. We show that no global stability estimates exist and any estimator converges arbitrarily slowly. We also provide further bounds on the instability generally complementing the analysis done in [arXiv:1907.03438]. Furthermore, we derive a decomposition of the discretized operator which allows us to study its injectivity and stability properties. It also leads to a faster implementation which we exploit in numerical experiments validating the instability estimates and the stability of the constrained problem.

AI.tldr: PINEM enables free-electron quantum state reconstruction from energy spectrograms via constrained linear inverse problem, but exhibits fundamental instability with arbitrarily slow convergence despite well-posedness.
AI.motivation: Recent PINEM advancements allow preparation and manipulation of free-electron quantum states, but reconstructing density matrices from electron energy spectrograms requires solving a challenging inverse problem with stability issues.
AI.method: Analyze well-posedness of constrained linear inverse problem for density matrix reconstruction, study stability properties, derive decomposition of discretized operator, and conduct numerical experiments to validate instability estimates.
AI.result: The problem is well-posed due to positive semi-definiteness constraint, but exhibits no global stability estimates - any estimator converges arbitrarily slowly. Numerical experiments validate these instability findings.
AI.conclusion: While PINEM quantum state reconstruction is theoretically well-posed, it suffers from fundamental instability with arbitrarily slow convergence, requiring careful consideration in practical implementations despite the regularizing effect of positivity constraints.

title: Mixing and enhanced dissipation in a time-translating shear flow
summary: Motivated in part by the work of Vanneste and Byatt-Smith, we study mixing and enhanced dissipation for the advection-diffusion equation with velocity field $\mathbf{u}(x,y,t)=(\sin(y-ct),0)$, a shear flow whose profile translates rigidly with speed $c$. This is a prototypical example of a flow whose critical points move in time. We quantify how the decay properties depend on the relation between translation speed $c$ and diffusivity $ν$. We first analyse the inviscid transport problem and establish time-averaged $H^{-1}$ mixing estimates for $t\lesssim c^{-1}$, yielding decay rates faster than stationary estimates. Building on these estimates, we prove enhanced dissipation for moderate translation speeds $c=c_0ν^\ell$ with $\ell\in(1/3,3/4)$. In this regime we obtain decay at rate $ν^{(1+2\ell)/5}$, which interpolates continuously between the sharp rates $ν^{1/2}$ for stationary shear flows with simple critical points and $ν^{1/3}$ for monotone flows. This quantifies how increasing translation speed progressively weakens the influence of the critical points. Comparing the inviscid mixing and enhanced dissipation timescales heuristically explains the lower endpoint $\ell=1/3$. For $c\gg 1$, we show that solutions remain close to those of the heat equation on fixed time intervals, such that the rapid translation averages out advection and weakens mixing. The mixing estimate relies on a refined stationary phase analysis exploiting cancellations generated by the motion of the critical points. The enhanced dissipation result requires an adaptation of the hypocoercivity framework for stationary shear flows to the non-autonomous setting. The translating flow prevents the commutator hierarchy from closing in the standard way, which we overcome by constructing an extended energy functional. The large-$c$ analysis exploits the averaging effect of rapid translations in this regime.

AI.tldr: Study of mixing and enhanced dissipation for advection-diffusion with translating shear flow u=(sin(y-ct),0), analyzing how decay depends on translation speed c vs diffusivity ν.
AI.motivation: Motivated by prior work of Vanneste and Byatt-Smith, study flows with moving critical points (unlike stationary flows), quantify how translation speed affects mixing and dissipation properties.
AI.method: Three regimes: 1) Inviscid transport analysis with time-averaged H⁻¹ mixing estimates; 2) Enhanced dissipation for moderate speeds c=c₀ν^ℓ using adapted hypocoercivity framework with extended energy functional; 3) Large-c analysis showing solutions approach heat equation via averaging effect.
AI.result: For moderate speeds (ℓ∈(1/3,3/4)), decay rate ν^{(1+2ℓ)/5} interpolating between ν^{1/2} (stationary shear) and ν^{1/3} (monotone flows). For large c, solutions resemble heat equation with weakened mixing. Lower endpoint ℓ=1/3 explained by comparing mixing and dissipation timescales.
AI.conclusion: Translation speed progressively weakens influence of critical points on mixing. Moderate speeds yield enhanced dissipation with interpolated rates, while rapid translation averages out advection. Analysis requires refined stationary phase and adapted hypocoercivity methods for non-autonomous flows.

title: A convolutional autoencoder and neural ODE framework for surrogate modeling of transient counterflow flames
comment: Submitted to the Proceedings of the Combustion Institute, Volume 42
summary: A novel convolutional autoencoder neural ODE (CAE-NODE) framework is proposed for a reduced-order model (ROM) of transient 2D counterflow flames, as an extension of AE-NODE methods in homogeneous reactive systems to spatially resolved flows. The spatial correlations of the multidimensional fields are extracted by the convolutional layers, allowing CAE to autonomously construct a physically consistent 6D continuous latent manifold by compressing high-fidelity 2D snapshots (256x256 grid, 21 variables) by over 100,000 times. The NODE is subsequently trained to describe the continuous-time dynamics on the non-linear manifold, enabling the prediction of the full temporal evolution of the flames by integrating forward in time from an initial condition. The results demonstrate that the network can accurately capture the entire transient process, including ignition, flame propagation, and the gradual transition to a non-premixed condition, with relative errors less than ~2% for major species. This study, for the first time, highlights the potential of CAE-NODE for surrogate modeling of unsteady dynamics of multi-dimensional reacting flows.

AI.tldr: CAE-NODE framework combines convolutional autoencoders with neural ODEs for reduced-order modeling of transient 2D counterflow flames, achieving 100,000x compression while maintaining ~2% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Extend AE-NODE methods from homogeneous reactive systems to spatially resolved flows, enabling surrogate modeling of unsteady dynamics in multi-dimensional reacting flows.
AI.method: Convolutional autoencoder extracts spatial correlations and compresses 2D snapshots (256x256 grid, 21 variables) to 6D latent manifold. Neural ODE learns continuous-time dynamics on this manifold for temporal evolution prediction.
AI.result: Accurately captures entire transient process (ignition, flame propagation, transition to non-premixed condition) with relative errors less than ~2% for major species.
AI.conclusion: First demonstration of CAE-NODE's potential for surrogate modeling of unsteady dynamics in multi-dimensional reacting flows with high compression and accuracy.

title: Trustworthy Koopman Operator Learning: Invariance Diagnostics and Error Bounds
summary: Koopman operator theory provides a global linear representation of nonlinear dynamics and underpins many data-driven methods. In practice, however, finite-dimensional feature spaces induced by a user-chosen dictionary are rarely invariant, so closure failures and projection errors lead to spurious eigenvalues, misleading Koopman modes, and overconfident forecasts. This paper addresses a central validation problem in data-driven Koopman methods: how to quantify invariance and projection errors for an arbitrary feature space using only snapshot data, and how to use these diagnostics to produce actionable guarantees and guide dictionary refinement? A unified a posteriori methodology is developed for certifying when a Koopman approximation is trustworthy and improving it when it is not. Koopman invariance is quantified using principal angles between a subspace and its Koopman image, yielding principal observables and a principal angle decomposition (PAD), a dynamics-informed alternative to SVD truncation with significantly improved performance. Multi-step error bounds are derived for Koopman and Perron--Frobenius mode decompositions, including RKHS-based pointwise guarantees, and are complemented by Gaussian process expected error surrogates. The resulting toolbox enables validated spectral analysis, certified forecasting, and principled dictionary and kernel learning, demonstrated on chaotic and high-dimensional benchmarks and real-world datasets, including cavity flow and the Pluto--Charon system.

AI.tldr: A posteriori validation framework for data-driven Koopman operator methods that quantifies invariance/projection errors, provides error bounds, and enables certified spectral analysis and forecasting.
AI.motivation: Finite-dimensional feature spaces in Koopman operator methods are rarely invariant, leading to closure failures, projection errors, spurious eigenvalues, misleading modes, and overconfident forecasts. Need to quantify these errors and provide actionable guarantees.
AI.method: Quantifies Koopman invariance using principal angles between subspace and its Koopman image, yielding principal observables and principal angle decomposition (PAD). Derives multi-step error bounds for Koopman and Perron-Frobenius mode decompositions with RKHS-based pointwise guarantees and Gaussian process expected error surrogates.
AI.result: Developed unified validation toolbox enabling certified spectral analysis, forecasting, and principled dictionary/kernel learning. Demonstrated on chaotic/high-dimensional benchmarks and real-world datasets including cavity flow and Pluto-Charon system.
AI.conclusion: Provides a posteriori methodology for certifying Koopman approximation trustworthiness and improving it when needed, addressing central validation problem in data-driven Koopman methods.

title: Energy cascade for cross-shear length scales in free-shear three-dimensional incompressible viscous flows
summary: The phenomenon of energy cascade is addressed in the case of free-shear flows, modeled with the equations for incompressible Newtonian fluids with mixed periodic and free-slip boundary conditions driven by an imposed mean shear profile. The rigorous results are proved for ensemble averages with respect to stationary statistical solutions in the sense of Foias and Prodi. We obtain the energy-budget relations with an energy dissipation term, a shear-production term from the mean flow, and an energy flux term of the fluctuation field, based on a decomposition of the flow into high and low horizontal wavenumber components, corresponding to scales perpendicular to the mean shear gradient. We estimate the shear-production term exploiting the orthogonality of horizontal Fourier modes and look for an energy cascade of the fluctuation flux. For any given wavenumber, we define an associated horizontal Taylor wavenumber of the high-wavenumber band above the given wavenumber. We prove two energy cascade results, for the energy flux and for a restricted energy flux that discounts possible energy loss to flow singularities, both valid for wavenumbers whose associated horizontal Taylor wavenumber lies above the viscous shear wavenumber and whose low-wavenumber energy dissipation rate is negligible compared to the total energy dissipation rate. On heuristic grounds, these rigorous conditions correspond precisely to an energy cascade in the range from the classical Corrsin scale down to the Kolmogorov dissipation scale, as expected in the conventional theory of turbulent shear flows.

AI.tldr: Rigorous mathematical proof of energy cascade in free-shear turbulent flows using statistical solutions and Fourier decomposition, establishing conditions for cascade from Corrsin to Kolmogorov scales.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous mathematical foundation for the phenomenon of energy cascade in free-shear turbulent flows, which has been widely accepted in conventional turbulence theory but lacks formal proof.
AI.method: Uses incompressible Newtonian fluid equations with mixed periodic/free-slip boundary conditions and imposed mean shear. Employs ensemble averages with stationary statistical solutions (Foias-Prodi), decomposes flow into high/low horizontal wavenumber components, defines energy budget relations with dissipation, shear-production, and flux terms, and introduces horizontal Taylor wavenumber concept.
AI.result: Proves two energy cascade results: one for energy flux and one for restricted energy flux (discounting energy loss to singularities). Both require wavenumbers whose associated horizontal Taylor wavenumber exceeds viscous shear wavenumber and where low-wavenumber dissipation is negligible compared to total dissipation.
AI.conclusion: The rigorous conditions correspond precisely to energy cascade in the range from Corrsin scale to Kolmogorov scale, providing mathematical validation for conventional turbulence theory of shear flows.

title: Consistent closure modeling in large eddy simulations by direct approximation of the filtered advection term
summary: This article addresses the widely overlooked conceptual inconsistency of the large eddy simulation (LES) framework, namely that the commonly used advection term introduces higher wave numbers in the filtered Navier-Stokes equations than consistent with the definition of a filtered equation. It is explained how this inconsistency is the reason that flux limiters, stabilization terms, or dealiasing is often required and that the LES solution is typically mesh dependent. A consistent alternative is the direct approximation of the filtered advection term, for which we derive an exact expression based on an infinite series expansion with terms of increasing order in the filter width. We show that truncating the series expansion after few terms gives an expression that is highly correlated with the filtered advection term and a suitable LES model. A posteriori studies with decaying turbulence and a turbulent shear flow are conducted that reveal that the proposed approximation of the filtered advection term predicts improved kinetic energy spectra and filtered velocity correlations compared to classical LES.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a conceptual inconsistency in LES framework where the advection term introduces higher wave numbers than allowed by filtered equations, causing mesh dependence and requiring stabilization. It proposes a consistent alternative by directly approximating the filtered advection term using an infinite series expansion.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the overlooked conceptual inconsistency in large eddy simulation (LES) where the commonly used advection term introduces higher wave numbers than consistent with filtered Navier-Stokes equations, leading to mesh dependence and requiring flux limiters, stabilization terms, or dealiasing.
AI.method: The method involves deriving an exact expression for the filtered advection term based on an infinite series expansion with terms of increasing order in filter width. The series is truncated after few terms to create a suitable LES model that directly approximates the filtered advection term consistently.
AI.result: A posteriori studies with decaying turbulence and turbulent shear flow show that the proposed approximation predicts improved kinetic energy spectra and filtered velocity correlations compared to classical LES approaches.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that directly approximating the filtered advection term through series expansion provides a consistent LES framework that eliminates the conceptual inconsistency of classical LES, reduces mesh dependence, and improves turbulence predictions without requiring additional stabilization techniques.

title: Generalized Tadmor Conditions and Structure-Preserving Numerical Fluxes for the Compressible Flow of Real Gases
summary: We generalize Tadmor's algebraic numerical flux condition for entropy-conservative discretizations of conservation laws to a broader class of secondary structures, i.e. possibly non-convex secondary quantities whose evolution can consist of both conservative and non-conservative contributions. The resulting generalized Tadmor condition yields a discrete local balance law for secondary structures alongside the discrete conservation law that is solved. In contrast to the convex entropy setting, non-convex secondary quantities can have singular Hessians and non-injective gradients; this introduces an additional necessary structural requirement, which we term (discrete) null-consistency. Null-consistency constrains admissible numerical work terms and is required for the existence and well-posedness of fluxes satisfying the generalized Tadmor condition. To construct such fluxes in practice, we show how discrete gradient operators provide systematic construction methods even when some of the functions entering the secondary structure are arbitrary, as in compressible flow closed by an arbitrary equation of state. As an application, we derive an entropy-conserving and kinetic-energy-consistent numerical flux for the Euler equations with an arbitrary (non-ideal) equation of state. We demonstrate the performance of the resulting scheme on a set of supercritical/transcritical compressible-flow test cases using several non-ideal equations of state, including a fully turbulent transcritical flow with a state-of-the-art equation of state and models for viscosity and heat conductivity. Computations are performed with our new open-source, flexible, JAX-based, multi-GPU compressible flow solver for Helmholtz-based equations of state available at github.com/rbklein/HelmEOS2.

AI.tldr: Generalizes Tadmor's entropy-conservative flux condition to non-convex secondary structures, introduces null-consistency requirement, and applies to Euler equations with arbitrary equations of state.
AI.motivation: To extend entropy-conservative discretizations beyond convex entropies to broader classes of secondary structures (including non-convex quantities) that appear in conservation laws, particularly for compressible flows with arbitrary equations of state.
AI.method: Generalizes Tadmor's algebraic flux condition to handle non-convex secondary structures, introduces discrete null-consistency as a structural requirement, uses discrete gradient operators for systematic flux construction, and applies to Euler equations with arbitrary equations of state.
AI.result: Develops entropy-conserving and kinetic-energy-consistent numerical flux for Euler equations with arbitrary non-ideal equations of state, demonstrates performance on supercritical/transcritical compressible-flow test cases including turbulent flows with state-of-the-art equations of state.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends entropy-conservative discretizations to non-convex secondary structures through generalized Tadmor condition with null-consistency, enabling robust numerical schemes for complex compressible flows with arbitrary equations of state.

title: Monotonicity for the fractional semi-linear problem in a half space
summary:
In this paper, we study semilinear fractional equations $$(-Δ)^s u(x) = f(u(x))$$ in a half-space and prove that all positive solutions are strictly increasing in the $x_n$-direction.
  Previous results typically require the solution $u$ to be globally bounded in $\mathbb{R}^n$. We substantially weaken this condition by assuming only that $u$ be bounded in each slab. Moreover, our analysis relies solely on the local Lipschitz continuity of the nonlinearity $f$, which is weaker than the conditions imposed in earlier works.
  As a crucial ingredient, we obtained a boundary H\"{older} regularity estimate that requires only the boundedness of $u$ near the boundary. This represents a significant improvement over existing results, which often assumed global boundedness of $u$ throughout $\mathbb{R}^n$. The proof introduces a new idea that may be of independent interest.
  To derive the monotonicity, we employ the method of moving planes. We first obtain a narrow region principle in unbounded domains, which ensures that the moving plane procedure can be initiated from $x_n = 0$. We then establish two averaging effects for the solutions to fractional equations. These key ingredients guarantee that the planes can be moved continuously all the way to $x_n = \infty$.
  Previously, narrow region principle can only be applied to a single narrow region. It is for the first time that we establish a multiple narrow region principle that can be applied simultaneously to finitely many narrow regions.
  Compared with the traditional approaches, methods based on the {\em averaging effect} require substantially weaker regularity assumptions and can even accommodate unbounded solutions.
  We believe that these new ideas and techniques develop here will serve as powerful tools in studying qualitative properties of solutions to fractional equations.

AI.tldr: The paper proves monotonicity of positive solutions to semilinear fractional equations in half-spaces using a novel averaging effect approach with weaker boundedness assumptions than previous works.
AI.motivation: Previous results on monotonicity of solutions to fractional equations required global boundedness assumptions. The authors aim to substantially weaken these conditions by only assuming boundedness in each slab, and to work with weaker regularity assumptions on the nonlinearity.
AI.method: The authors use the method of moving planes with several key innovations: (1) a boundary Hölder regularity estimate requiring only local boundedness, (2) a narrow region principle for unbounded domains, (3) two averaging effects for fractional equations, and (4) a novel multiple narrow region principle that can handle finitely many narrow regions simultaneously.
AI.result: All positive solutions to semilinear fractional equations in a half-space are strictly increasing in the x_n-direction under much weaker conditions: only requiring u to be bounded in each slab (not globally bounded) and f to be locally Lipschitz continuous.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces powerful new techniques for studying qualitative properties of fractional equations, particularly the averaging effect approach that requires substantially weaker regularity assumptions and can handle unbounded solutions. These methods represent significant improvements over traditional approaches.

title: Hamiltonian dynamics for stochastic reconstruction in emission tomography
summary: The AMIAS/RISE framework formulates emission tomography as a probabilistic inverse problem in which reconstructed images are sampled from a distribution defined by the measurement model and counting statistics. In this work we present a stochastic reformulation of this approach based on gradient-driven optimization combined with Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) sampling directly in high-dimensional voxel space. This formulation enables practical ensemble generation for tomographic image reconstructions and provides direct access to image fluctuations within the sampled ensemble. Beyond point reconstruction, we introduce a spatially resolved operator-weighted diagnostic-the sampled data-visible variance-which quantifies how image fluctuations propagate through the imaging operator and thereby probes the local conditioning of the inverse problem under realistic acquisition physics. Using controlled software phantoms, experimental anthropomorphic phantom measurements, and a clinical DATSCAN SPECT acquisition, we demonstrate that while point-estimate accuracy under ideal conditions is comparable to deterministic reconstruction methods, the stochastic formulation provides additional physically interpretable insight. In particular, the ensemble analysis distinguishes uncertainty arising from intrinsic ill-posedness of the inverse problem from that associated with forward-model inadequacy. The clinical example is included to illustrate methodological applicability under realistic acquisition statistics rather than to assess diagnostic performance. The results establish the stochastic reconstruction framework as a practical ensemble-based approach for uncertainty quantification and forward-model validation in emission tomography.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a stochastic reconstruction framework for emission tomography using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling to generate image ensembles and quantify uncertainty, distinguishing between inverse problem ill-posedness and forward-model inadequacy.
AI.motivation: Current emission tomography reconstruction methods typically provide only point estimates without uncertainty quantification. There's a need for practical ensemble-based approaches that can quantify image uncertainty and distinguish between different sources of uncertainty (inverse problem ill-posedness vs. forward-model inadequacy).
AI.method: The AMIAS/RISE framework is reformulated using gradient-driven optimization combined with Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) sampling directly in high-dimensional voxel space. This enables ensemble generation and introduces a spatially resolved operator-weighted diagnostic called "sampled data-visible variance" that quantifies how image fluctuations propagate through the imaging operator.
AI.result: The method was validated using software phantoms, experimental anthropomorphic phantom measurements, and clinical DATSCAN SPECT acquisition. While point-estimate accuracy under ideal conditions is comparable to deterministic methods, the stochastic formulation provides additional physically interpretable insight by distinguishing uncertainty sources and enabling forward-model validation.
AI.conclusion: The stochastic reconstruction framework establishes a practical ensemble-based approach for uncertainty quantification and forward-model validation in emission tomography, providing valuable diagnostic tools beyond traditional point reconstruction methods.

title: Structure-preserving preconditioning of discrete space-fractional diffusion equations with variable coefficient and θ-Method
summary:
This paper studies the spectral properties of large matrices and the preconditioning of linear systems, arising from the finite difference discretization of a time-dependent space-fractional diffusion equation with a variable coefficient $a(x)$ defined on $Ω\subset \mathbb{R}^d$, $d=1,2$. The model involves a one-sided Riemann-Liouville fractional derivative multiplied by the function $a(x)$, discretized by the shifted Gr"unwald formula in space and the $θ$-method in time. The resulting all-at-once linear systems exhibit a $(d+1)$-level Toeplitz-like matrix structure, with $d=1,2$ denoting the space dimension, while the additional level is due to the time variable.
  A preconditioning strategy is developed based on the structural properties of the discretized operator. Using the generalized locally Toeplitz (GLT) theory, we analyze the spectral distribution of the unpreconditioned and preconditioned matrix sequences. The main novelty is that the analysis fully covers the case where the variable coefficient $a$ is nonconstant. Numerical results are provided to support the GLT based theoretical findings, and some possible extensions are briefly discussed.

AI.tldr: Analysis of spectral properties and preconditioning for matrices from space-fractional diffusion equations with variable coefficients, using GLT theory to handle nonconstant coefficients.
AI.motivation: To study spectral properties and develop effective preconditioning strategies for large linear systems arising from discretization of time-dependent space-fractional diffusion equations with variable coefficients, which is challenging when the coefficient is nonconstant.
AI.method: Discretization using shifted Grünwald formula for space-fractional derivatives and θ-method in time, resulting in (d+1)-level Toeplitz-like matrices. Preconditioning strategy based on structural properties, analyzed using generalized locally Toeplitz (GLT) theory to handle nonconstant coefficients.
AI.result: Developed preconditioning strategy for variable coefficient case, analyzed spectral distribution of unpreconditioned/preconditioned matrices using GLT theory, with numerical results supporting theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: GLT theory provides effective framework for analyzing spectral properties and preconditioning of matrices from space-fractional diffusion equations, even with nonconstant coefficients, enabling efficient solution of resulting linear systems.

title: Nonlinear Wave Dynamics in Black Hole Spacetimes
summary:
This manuscript is a lightly reformatted version of my 2017 PhD thesis. I am posting it on arXiv at the request of my advisor, Sergiu Klainerman, who noted that it has been useful to some students. The content largely reflects the thesis in its original form.
  This thesis details a method for proving global boundedness and decay results for nonlinear wave equations on black hole spacetimes. The method is applied to five example problems of increasing difficulty. The first problem, which addresses the semilinear wave equation on Minkowski space, is quite simple and should be accessible to a reader who is still new to the field of partial differential equations. The final problem, which was posed by Ionescu and Klainerman in [IK14], constitutes a step toward proving stability for slowly rotating Kerr black holes. The remaining intermediate problems are: a semilinear wave equation on the Schwarzschild spacetime, a semilinear wave equation on any subextremal Kerr spacetime with the additional assumption of axisymmetry, and a restriction of the final problem to the Schwarzschild case.
  The method used in this thesis is based on a few particular developments that may be useful for other related problems. These include: a new method for constructing Morawetz-type estimates that is fairly robust (insofar as it may be successfully applied to all five problems), a strategy based on a decay hierarchy for energy estimates on uniformly spacelike hypersurfaces using, in particular, a notion of weak decay, and a technique for handling certain terms with factors that are singular on an axis of symmetry.

AI.tldr: A PhD thesis presenting a method for proving global boundedness and decay for nonlinear wave equations on black hole spacetimes, applied to five progressively harder problems from Minkowski space to slowly rotating Kerr black holes.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust method for analyzing nonlinear wave equations on black hole spacetimes, addressing stability problems in general relativity and making progress toward proving stability for slowly rotating Kerr black holes.
AI.method: Uses a novel approach with: 1) a new robust method for constructing Morawetz-type estimates applicable to all problems, 2) a decay hierarchy strategy for energy estimates on uniformly spacelike hypersurfaces using weak decay concepts, and 3) techniques for handling terms singular on symmetry axes.
AI.result: Successfully applies the method to five example problems of increasing difficulty: from simple semilinear wave equations on Minkowski space to complex problems on Schwarzschild and Kerr spacetimes, making progress toward stability proofs for slowly rotating Kerr black holes.
AI.conclusion: The thesis presents a comprehensive, robust method for analyzing nonlinear wave equations on black hole backgrounds, with techniques that are broadly applicable to related problems in mathematical general relativity and partial differential equations.

title: ANNA: a toolbox for Newtonian Noise Analysis
comment: Preprint submitted to the Journal of Computational Physics
summary:
The Einstein Telescope (ET) is a third-generation underground gravitational wave observatory designed to achieve an unprecedented sensitivity down to 3 Hz. Waves propagating in the soil due to anthropogenic or natural vibration sources generate density fluctuations which cause gravitational attraction, resulting in motion of the mirrors of the laser interferometer known as Newtonian noise. The latter is computed by integrating density fluctuations due to seismic wave fields over the soil domain surrounding the test mass.
  ANNA Newtonian Noise Analysis is a toolbox that computes Newtonian Noise from a seismic wave field defined on a finite element mesh, using Gaussian quadrature. 3D finite element meshes composed of linear and quadratic tetrahedral (4-node and 10-node) and brick (8-node and 20-node) elements are supported. The user computes (or interpolates) a seismic wave field on a finite element mesh and the toolbox computes the total Newtonian noise, as well as the bulk and surface contributions. ANNA runs in the MATLAB programming and numeric computing platform and is compatible with the open-source GNU Octave Scientific Programming Language; a Python version is also available.
  The toolbox is verified for plane P- and S-waves propagating in an elastic homogeneous full space with a mirror suspended in a spherical cavity, assuming that the wavelength is much larger than the radius of the cavity, so that wave scattering can be ignored. Excellent agreement with analytical solutions is obtained. Similar good agreement is reported for the Newtonian noise on a test mass suspended at a finite distance above the free surface of a homogeneous elastic halfspace in which a Rayleigh wave propagates.
  The proposed finite element framework provides a physically consistent and computationally efficient approach for computing gravitational-seismic coupling in heterogeneous media.

AI.tldr: ANNA is a MATLAB/Octave/Python toolbox for computing Newtonian noise in gravitational wave detectors using finite element analysis of seismic wave fields.
AI.motivation: Third-generation gravitational wave observatories like Einstein Telescope need to mitigate Newtonian noise caused by seismic vibrations affecting mirrors, requiring accurate modeling in heterogeneous media.
AI.method: Finite element analysis using Gaussian quadrature on 3D meshes (tetrahedral/brick elements) to compute gravitational attraction from seismic density fluctuations around test masses.
AI.result: Toolbox verified with excellent agreement to analytical solutions for plane P-/S-waves in homogeneous full space and Rayleigh waves in halfspace; provides bulk/surface noise contributions.
AI.conclusion: ANNA offers physically consistent and computationally efficient framework for gravitational-seismic coupling analysis in heterogeneous media for next-generation gravitational wave detectors.

title: An Efficient Cumulative Edge-Detection Method for Image Reconstruction
summary:
When reconstructing images from noisy measurements, such as in medical scans or scientific imaging, we face an inverse problem: recovering an unknown image from indirect, corrupted observations. These problems are typically ill-posed, meaning small amounts of noise can lead to inaccurate reconstructions. Regularization techniques address this by incorporating prior assumptions about the solution, such as smoothness or sparsity. However, standard methods often blur sharp edges--the boundaries between tissues or structures--losing critical detail.
  A powerful strategy for edge preservation is iterative reweighting, which solves a sequence of weighted subproblems with adaptively updated weights. Non-cumulative schemes derive weights from the current iterate alone and can be solved efficiently using the Recycled Majorization-Minimization Generalized Krylov Subspace method (RMM-GKS). The cumulative approach of Gazzola et al. progressively accumulates edge information across iterations, achieving superior edge preservation but at high computational cost.
  This work introduces CR-$\ell_q$-RMM-GKS, which combines cumulative edge detection with computational efficiency. We integrate Gazzola's cumulative weighting with RMM-GKS, which handles general $\ell_q$ penalties ($0 < q \le 2$), automatically selects regularization parameters, and recycles Krylov subspaces between iterations, reducing the nested structure to two levels.
  Numerical experiments in signal deblurring and tomography demonstrate that CR-$\ell_q$-RMM-GKS produces significantly sharper edge reconstructions than standard non-cumulative methods. In particular, CR-$\ell_1$-RMM-GKS outperforms both standard $\ell_1$ methods and CR-$\ell_2$-RMM-GKS, indicating that cumulative weighting and $\ell_1$ penalties are highly complementary.

AI.tldr: CR-ℓq-RMM-GKS combines cumulative edge detection with computational efficiency for image reconstruction, outperforming standard methods in edge preservation.
AI.motivation: Standard regularization methods for ill-posed inverse problems (like medical imaging) often blur sharp edges, losing critical structural details. While cumulative edge detection preserves edges better, it's computationally expensive.
AI.method: Integrates Gazzola's cumulative weighting with RMM-GKS framework, which handles ℓq penalties (0<q≤2), automatically selects regularization parameters, and recycles Krylov subspaces between iterations to reduce computational complexity.
AI.result: CR-ℓq-RMM-GKS produces significantly sharper edge reconstructions than standard non-cumulative methods in signal deblurring and tomography. CR-ℓ1-RMM-GKS outperforms both standard ℓ1 methods and CR-ℓ2-RMM-GKS.
AI.conclusion: Cumulative weighting combined with ℓ1 penalties is highly complementary for edge preservation, and the proposed method achieves superior edge reconstruction while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Localized Enhanced Dissipation: A Hypocoercivity Approach
summary: In this paper, we consider the passive scalar solutions in shear flows with critical points. With a detailed hypocoercivity functional, we develop streamline-wise enhanced dissipation estimates.

AI.tldr: Passive scalar solutions in shear flows with critical points analyzed using hypocoercivity functional to obtain enhanced dissipation estimates.
AI.motivation: To understand the behavior of passive scalar solutions in shear flows with critical points, which are important for studying mixing and transport phenomena in fluid dynamics.
AI.method: Developed a detailed hypocoercivity functional to analyze the system and derived streamline-wise enhanced dissipation estimates.
AI.result: Obtained enhanced dissipation estimates for passive scalar solutions in shear flows with critical points through hypocoercivity analysis.
AI.conclusion: The hypocoercivity approach provides effective tools for studying dissipation in shear flows with critical points, offering insights into mixing properties.

title: Adjoint-Based Gradient Evaluation for Metasurface Inverse Design via Affine Geometric Transformations
summary:
The sharp increasing in fabrication capabilities of nanomaterials, and complex structures such as meta-surfaces and metalens, has opened to the possibility of employing them for accurately control the electromagnetic field, beyond the possibility ensured by traditional devices.
  The demand for large scale structures and more complex functionalities from meta-surfaces lead to the research for advanced techniques of inverse design, able to conjugate the ability to produce effective designs and limited computational cost. Among the various approaches for inverse design of large meta-surfaces, the ones based on the adjoint variable method are appealing since able to ensure a minimal computational cost for the gradient computation of the cost function.
  In this work, a systematic methodology for the application of the adjoint variable method for large meta-surface design is presented. The method is based on: (i) a parametrization of the relevant geometric parameters of the meta-atoms, (ii) the fast computation of the gradient with respect such parameters, allowing for the implementation of general affine transformations during the optimization process.
  The main findings are first theoretically justified and a numerical validation is provided to show the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: A systematic methodology using adjoint variable method for efficient inverse design of large meta-surfaces with minimal computational cost for gradient computation.
AI.motivation: The increasing fabrication capabilities for nanomaterials and complex structures like meta-surfaces/metalens enable precise electromagnetic field control beyond traditional devices. There's demand for large-scale structures with complex functionalities, requiring advanced inverse design techniques that balance effectiveness with computational efficiency.
AI.method: Systematic methodology based on adjoint variable method with: (i) parametrization of meta-atom geometric parameters, (ii) fast gradient computation with respect to these parameters, enabling implementation of general affine transformations during optimization.
AI.result: Theoretical justification of main findings and numerical validation demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach for large meta-surface design.
AI.conclusion: The adjoint variable method provides an appealing approach for inverse design of large meta-surfaces due to its minimal computational cost for gradient computation, enabling efficient optimization of complex electromagnetic structures.

title: Quantitative Closure Analysis toward Ideal Fluids
comment: 146 pages, This arXiv preprint is the detailed companion to the compact journal-submission version of the same title and is posted on arXiv.org for readers' convenience. This version is not intended for separate journal publication
summary: We establish the incompressible low--Mach/high--Reynolds limit for the Boltzmann equation for a broad class of initial data, without recourse to any asymptotic expansion. Exploiting the local Maxwellian manifold and the macro--micro decomposition, together with dispersive control of the acoustic component, we obtain quantitative estimates on the purely microscopic fluctuation, as well as a control of the entropic fluctuation and a kinetic vorticity in terms of their initial data. As a consequence, in two space dimensions the rescaled velocity and temperature converge to a global solution in the sense of DiPerna--Lions--Majda and Delort of the incompressible Euler equations with an advected temperature.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes the incompressible low-Mach/high-Reynolds limit for the Boltzmann equation without asymptotic expansions, showing convergence to incompressible Euler equations with temperature advection in 2D.
AI.motivation: To rigorously establish the connection between kinetic theory (Boltzmann equation) and fluid dynamics (incompressible Euler equations) in the low-Mach/high-Reynolds number regime, providing a mathematical foundation for this important physical limit.
AI.method: Uses local Maxwellian manifold and macro-micro decomposition, with dispersive control of acoustic components. Obtains quantitative estimates on microscopic fluctuations, entropic fluctuations, and kinetic vorticity in terms of initial data.
AI.result: In two space dimensions, the rescaled velocity and temperature converge to a global solution (in DiPerna-Lions-Majda and Delort sense) of the incompressible Euler equations with advected temperature.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework for deriving incompressible fluid equations from kinetic theory without relying on asymptotic expansions, establishing convergence to global solutions in 2D.

title: UniMatSim: A High-Throughput Materials Simulation Automation Framework Based on Universal Machine Learning Potentials
comment: 14 pages main text, 17 pages Supporting Information, 4 figures
summary: Universal machine learning interatomic potentials (UMLIPs) offer accuracy close to first-principles calculations at a fraction of the cost, showing significant potential for large-scale material simulations. However, the fragmented UMLIPs ecosystem lacks unified interface standards and integration frameworks, hindering their automated deployment in high-throughput workflows. To address this, we present UniMatSim, a modular Python framework. It systematically integrates various UMLIPs (e.g., CHGNet, M3GNet, MACE) and automates workflows from structural optimization to stability verification. The framework enables seamless model switching via abstracted interfaces, incorporates task orchestration, and provides standardized modules for key properties (elasticity, phonons, molecular dynamics), including automated handling for low-dimensional materials. As a test case, using the 2D Lieb lattice system, we constructed a multi-stage high-throughput screening workflow covering structural optimization, elastic stability, and phonon spectrum calculations. Starting from 1,176 candidate compositions, a four-model consensus pipeline yields 393 stable structures. These are refined by magnetic-state screening and DFT band-structure calculations to 59 Lieb-lattice candidates with staggered-magnetic-band characteristics. Results show UniMatSim significantly improves computational efficiency and reproducibility, providing a reliable infrastructure for data-driven materials discovery and design.

AI.tldr: UniMatSim is a modular Python framework that unifies fragmented universal machine learning interatomic potentials (UMLIPs) ecosystem, enabling automated high-throughput materials simulations with standardized interfaces and workflow orchestration.
AI.motivation: The fragmented UMLIPs ecosystem lacks unified interface standards and integration frameworks, hindering automated deployment in high-throughput materials simulation workflows despite UMLIPs offering near-first-principles accuracy at lower computational cost.
AI.method: Developed UniMatSim, a modular Python framework that systematically integrates various UMLIPs (CHGNet, M3GNet, MACE) with abstracted interfaces for seamless model switching, task orchestration, and standardized modules for key material properties including elasticity, phonons, and molecular dynamics with automated handling for low-dimensional materials.
AI.result: Demonstrated with 2D Lieb lattice system: starting from 1,176 candidate compositions, a four-model consensus pipeline identified 393 stable structures, refined to 59 Lieb-lattice candidates with staggered-magnetic-band characteristics after magnetic-state screening and DFT band-structure calculations. UniMatSim significantly improved computational efficiency and reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: UniMatSim provides a reliable infrastructure for data-driven materials discovery and design by unifying the fragmented UMLIPs ecosystem, enabling automated high-throughput workflows with improved computational efficiency and reproducibility.

title: Multilevel Sparse Tensor Approximation for High-Dimensional Parametric PDEs
summary: In this paper the efficiency of multilevel sparse tensor approximation methods for high-dimensional affine parametric diffusion equations is investigated. Methodologically, the recently presented Sparse Alternating Least Squares (SALS) algorithm is employed to construct adaptive tensor train (TT) approximations of quantities of interest (QoI). By combining this tensor-based approach with a multilevel Galerkin discretization strategy, the solution's regularity can be exploited to significantly reduce computational costs by level-adapted sample sizes. A rigorous theoretical analysis is derived, demonstrating that the work overhead for the proposed multilevel method remains independent of the discretization level, which stands in stark contrast to the exponential growth observed in single-level approaches. The presented analysis is quite general and not constrained to the sparse TT format but uses a generic framework that can be extended to other model classes. Numerical experiments validate the predicted efficiency gains in high-dimensional settings.

AI.tldr: Multilevel sparse tensor approximation methods for high-dimensional parametric PDEs achieve computational efficiency with level-adapted sampling, avoiding exponential cost growth.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional parametric diffusion equations suffer from exponential computational cost growth in traditional single-level approaches, requiring more efficient methods.
AI.method: Combines Sparse Alternating Least Squares (SALS) algorithm for adaptive tensor train approximations with multilevel Galerkin discretization using level-adapted sample sizes.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows work overhead remains independent of discretization level (vs exponential growth in single-level), validated by numerical experiments in high-dimensional settings.
AI.conclusion: Multilevel sparse tensor approximation provides efficient solution for high-dimensional parametric PDEs with general framework applicable beyond tensor train format.

title: On the stability of multi-dimensional rarefaction waves with vorticity
summary: We study the stability of centered rarefaction waves for 2D isentropic compressible Euler equations for polytropic gas. The proof relies on the energy estimates on acoustical waves and vorticity wave simultaneously \emph{without loss of derivatives} in rarefaction wave region via reformulating the Euler equation as a wave-transport system introduced by Luk-Speck in \cite{LukSpeck2D}, which removes the irrotational assumption of Luo-Yu \cite{Luo-YuRare1, Luo-YuRare2}. As applications, we show that the local existence of piecewise continuous solutions to the Riemann problem in the perturbative regime of \emph{shock wave-rarefaction wave} and \emph{rarefaction wave-vortex sheet-rarefaction wave}.

AI.tldr: The paper proves stability of centered rarefaction waves for 2D isentropic compressible Euler equations for polytropic gas without loss of derivatives, and applies this to local existence of piecewise continuous solutions for shock-rarefaction and rarefaction-vortex sheet-rarefaction Riemann problems.
AI.motivation: To establish stability results for centered rarefaction waves in 2D compressible Euler equations without the irrotational assumption, enabling analysis of more complex wave interactions in Riemann problems.
AI.method: Uses energy estimates on acoustical waves and vorticity wave simultaneously without loss of derivatives via reformulating Euler equations as a wave-transport system (Luk-Speck approach), removing the irrotational assumption from previous work.
AI.result: Proves stability of centered rarefaction waves for 2D isentropic compressible Euler equations, and shows local existence of piecewise continuous solutions for shock wave-rarefaction wave and rarefaction wave-vortex sheet-rarefaction wave Riemann problems in perturbative regime.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes stability results for rarefaction waves without irrotational assumptions and extends applicability to more complex wave interactions in compressible fluid dynamics.

title: Ab Initio Study of Erbium Point Defects in 4H-SiC for Quantum Devices
summary: Identifying scalable materials systems that exhibit quantum behavior is a central challenge in quantum information science. Point defects in certain wide-bandgap semiconductors are promising in this regard due to the maturity of semiconductor manufacturing and ion implantation technology. Single erbium defect centers in 4H-SiC are examples of such defects that provide access to discrete defect-induced electron energy levels within the bulk material bandgap, which can be utilized for a variety of quantum technologies, such as single-photon emission for secure communication and distributed quantum computing. This work presents a first-principles study of erbium point defects in 4H-SiC using density functional theory. These results provide materials-level support for the development of Er point defects in 4H-SiC as a scalable platform for quantum devices, helping to bridge the gap between quantum physics and the practical realization of quantum networks.

AI.tldr: First-principles DFT study of erbium point defects in 4H-SiC shows they provide discrete defect-induced electron energy levels within the bandgap, supporting their development as a scalable quantum platform.
AI.motivation: To identify scalable materials systems with quantum behavior for quantum information science, leveraging mature semiconductor manufacturing and ion implantation technology.
AI.method: First-principles study using density functional theory (DFT) to analyze erbium point defects in 4H-SiC.
AI.result: Erbium defects in 4H-SiC provide discrete defect-induced electron energy levels within the bulk material bandgap, enabling applications like single-photon emission for quantum technologies.
AI.conclusion: Er point defects in 4H-SiC offer a promising scalable platform for quantum devices, bridging quantum physics with practical quantum network realization.

title: A scaled TW-PINN: A physics-informed neural network for traveling wave solutions of reaction-diffusion equations with general coefficients
summary: We propose an efficient and generalizable physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework for computing traveling wave solutions of $n$-dimensional reaction-diffusion equations with various reaction and diffusion coefficients. By applying a scaling transformation with the traveling wave form, the original problem is reduced to a one-dimensional scaled reaction-diffusion equation with unit reaction and diffusion coefficients. This reduction leads to the proposed framework, termed scaled TW-PINN, in which a single PINN solver trained on the scaled equation is reused for different coefficient choices and spatial dimensions. We also prove a universal approximation property of the proposed PINN solver for traveling wave solutions. Numerical experiments in one and two dimensions, together with a comparison to the existing wave-PINN method, demonstrate the accuracy, flexibility, and superior performance of scaled TW-PINN. Finally, we explore an extension of the framework to the Fisher's equation with general initial conditions.

AI.tldr: Scaled TW-PINN: A physics-informed neural network framework that uses scaling transformations to compute traveling wave solutions for n-dimensional reaction-diffusion equations with varying coefficients, enabling reuse of a single solver across different parameter settings.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for computing traveling wave solutions of reaction-diffusion equations often require separate solvers for different reaction/diffusion coefficients and spatial dimensions, leading to computational inefficiency and lack of generalizability.
AI.method: Apply scaling transformation with traveling wave form to reduce n-dimensional reaction-diffusion equations to a one-dimensional scaled equation with unit coefficients. Train a single PINN solver on this scaled equation that can be reused for different coefficient choices and spatial dimensions.
AI.result: Numerical experiments in 1D and 2D demonstrate accuracy, flexibility, and superior performance compared to existing wave-PINN method. The framework also shows potential for extension to Fisher's equation with general initial conditions.
AI.conclusion: Scaled TW-PINN provides an efficient, generalizable framework for traveling wave solutions that reduces computational cost by enabling reuse of a single trained solver across various parameter settings and dimensions.

title: Towards a Gagliardo-Type Theory of Fractional Sobolev Spaces on Arbitrary Time Scales
summary: We introduce a Gagliardo-type approach to fractional Sobolev spaces on arbitrary time scales, based on the Lebesgue $Δ$-measure and on nonlocal interaction energies defined on the off-diagonal set $Ω_T \subset T \times T$. For constant fractional order $α\in (0,1)$ and $1 \le p < \infty$, we define the associated fractional seminorm and the corresponding space $W^{α,p}_Δ(T)$, and we show that this construction is well posed in the measure-theoretic setting of time scales. In particular, we establish that these spaces are Banach spaces for all $1 \le p < \infty$, reflexive for $1 < p < \infty$, and Hilbert spaces in the case $p=2$. We then prove a Poincaré-type inequality on a natural class of bounded hybrid time scales, showing that the geometry of the underlying time scale enters the theory already at the level of coercive estimates. The construction recovers, within a unified framework, the continuous, discrete, and hybrid settings. We conclude by briefly indicating a possible extension to variable-order nonlocal spaces, which will be studied separately.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces fractional Sobolev spaces on arbitrary time scales using a Gagliardo-type approach based on nonlocal interaction energies, establishing their functional analytic properties and proving a Poincaré-type inequality.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified framework for fractional Sobolev spaces that works across continuous, discrete, and hybrid time scales, addressing the need for a measure-theoretic approach to fractional calculus on arbitrary time domains.
AI.method: A Gagliardo-type approach using Lebesgue Δ-measure and nonlocal interaction energies defined on the off-diagonal set Ω_T ⊂ T × T, constructing fractional seminorms and spaces W^{α,p}_Δ(T) for constant fractional order α∈(0,1) and 1≤p<∞.
AI.result: The spaces W^{α,p}_Δ(T) are Banach spaces for all 1≤p<∞, reflexive for 1<p<∞, and Hilbert spaces for p=2. A Poincaré-type inequality is proved on bounded hybrid time scales, showing geometric dependence.
AI.conclusion: The construction provides a unified framework for fractional Sobolev spaces across continuous, discrete, and hybrid settings, with potential extensions to variable-order nonlocal spaces.

title: Building Trust in PINNs: Error Estimation through Finite Difference Methods
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) constitute a flexible deep learning approach for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), which model phenomena ranging from heat conduction to quantum mechanical systems. Despite their flexibility, PINNs offer limited insight into how their predictions deviate from the true solution, hindering trust in their prediction quality. We propose a lightweight post-hoc method that addresses this gap by producing pointwise error estimates for PINN predictions, which offer a natural form of explanation for such models, identifying not just whether a prediction is wrong, but where and by how much. For linear partial differential equations, the error between a PINN approximation and the true solution satisfies the same differential operator as the original problem, but driven by the PINN's PDE residual as its source term. We solve this error equation numerically using finite difference methods requiring no knowledge of the true solution. Evaluated on several benchmark PDEs, our method yields accurate error maps at low computational cost, enabling targeted and interpretable validation of PINNs.

AI.tldr: A lightweight post-hoc method produces pointwise error estimates for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) predictions by solving an error equation using finite difference methods, enabling interpretable validation of PINN accuracy.
AI.motivation: PINNs provide limited insight into how their predictions deviate from true solutions, hindering trust in prediction quality. There's a need for methods that can identify not just whether predictions are wrong, but where and by how much they deviate.
AI.method: For linear PDEs, the error between PINN approximation and true solution satisfies the same differential operator as the original problem, driven by the PINN's PDE residual as source term. This error equation is solved numerically using finite difference methods without requiring knowledge of the true solution.
AI.result: The method yields accurate error maps at low computational cost when evaluated on several benchmark PDEs, enabling targeted and interpretable validation of PINNs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight post-hoc method provides pointwise error estimates for PINN predictions, offering a natural form of explanation that identifies where and by how much predictions deviate from true solutions, thereby enhancing trust in PINN prediction quality.

title: FFT-accelerated computation of the Dirichlet-to-Neumann map for inhomogeneous exterior Helmholtz problems using the method of fundamental solutions
summary: An efficient numerical method is proposed for computing the Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) map associated with the exterior Dirichlet problem for the two-dimensional Helmholtz equation with an inhomogeneous term. The exterior solution is approximated by the method of fundamental solutions (MFS). When the source and collocation points are equally spaced on concentric circles, the coefficient matrices arising in the discretization become circulant, which enables efficient evaluation of the discrete DtN map by the fast Fourier transform (FFT). By incorporating the boundary condition defined by the DtN map into a finite element formulation, the original exterior problem posed on an unbounded domain is reduced to an equivalent problem on a bounded computational domain, which can then be solved by the finite element method (FEM). Numerical examples show that the proposed MFS-based approach computes the DtN map with high accuracy, and that the FEM incorporating the boundary condition defined by the DtN map yields accurate solutions for exterior Helmholtz problems with an inhomogeneous term posed on unbounded domains.

AI.tldr: An efficient FFT-based MFS method for computing DtN maps for 2D Helmholtz exterior problems with inhomogeneous terms, enabling accurate FEM solutions on bounded domains.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient numerical method for solving exterior Helmholtz problems with inhomogeneous terms on unbounded domains by computing the Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) map accurately and efficiently.
AI.method: Uses Method of Fundamental Solutions (MFS) with source and collocation points equally spaced on concentric circles, creating circulant matrices that enable FFT-based efficient DtN computation. The DtN map is then incorporated as a boundary condition in FEM to reduce unbounded domain problems to bounded computational domains.
AI.result: The MFS-based approach computes DtN maps with high accuracy, and FEM incorporating these DtN boundary conditions yields accurate solutions for exterior Helmholtz problems with inhomogeneous terms on unbounded domains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully combines MFS with FFT acceleration for efficient DtN computation and integrates this with FEM to solve exterior Helmholtz problems with inhomogeneous terms effectively on bounded computational domains.

title: Local Existence and Finite-Time Singularity Formation in the Vlasov-Poisson-Isotropic Landau System
summary:
The isotropic Landau (Coulomb) operator was introduced in kinetic theory by Krieger and Strain (Comm. Partial Differential Equations, 2012). In this work, we study the spatially inhomogeneous Vlasov--Poisson--isotropic Landau system. We first establish a local--in--time existence theory for the Cauchy problem: for initial data satisfying a suitable smallness condition in an appropriate norm, there exists a non--negative solution on a time interval $[0,T]$, where the lifespan $T$ depends on the size of the initial data.
  Beyond the local theory, we investigate a mechanism that may lead to the breakdown of global existence. We show that finite--time singularity formation can occur in the gravitationally attractive case, provided that the weak solution satisfies certain a priori regularity and decay assumptions, the initial gravitational field energy exceeds the kinetic energy, and the resulting energy gap dominates the diffusive effect of the collision operator. As a consequence, if the solution is further assumed to belong to a suitable measure space up to the maximal existence time, it collapses to a single point in physical space at that time. The proof of finite--time singularity formation is based on deriving an upper bound for the second spatial moment, which becomes negative in finite time.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the Vlasov-Poisson-isotropic Landau system, establishing local existence for small initial data and proving finite-time singularity formation in the gravitational case under specific conditions.
AI.motivation: To extend the analysis of the isotropic Landau operator from the spatially homogeneous setting to the spatially inhomogeneous Vlasov-Poisson-isotropic Landau system, investigating both local well-posedness and potential breakdown mechanisms for global existence.
AI.method: First establishes local-in-time existence theory for small initial data using appropriate functional norms. For singularity analysis, derives an upper bound for the second spatial moment and shows it becomes negative in finite time under conditions where gravitational field energy exceeds kinetic energy and dominates diffusion.
AI.result: Local existence proved for small initial data. Finite-time singularity formation demonstrated in gravitational case when: (1) solution satisfies regularity/decay assumptions, (2) initial gravitational field energy > kinetic energy, (3) energy gap dominates diffusion. Solutions collapse to a point in physical space at maximal existence time.
AI.conclusion: The Vlasov-Poisson-isotropic Landau system admits local solutions for small data, but global existence can break down through finite-time singularity formation in the gravitational case when attractive forces dominate diffusive effects, leading to point collapse.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Systems for the Simulation of EUV Electromagnetic Wave Diffraction from a Lithography Mask
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2507.04153
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and neural operators (NOs) for solving the problem of diffraction of Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) electromagnetic waves from contemporary lithography masks are presented. A novel hybrid Waveguide Neural Operator (WGNO) is introduced, based on a waveguide method with its most computationally expensive components replaced by a neural network. To evaluate performance, the accuracy and inference time of PINNs and NOs are compared against modern numerical solvers for a series of problems with known exact solutions. The emphasis is placed on investigation of solution accuracy by considered artificial neural systems for 13.5 nm and 11.2 nm wavelengths. Numerical experiments on realistic 2D and 3D masks demonstrate that PINNs and neural operators achieve competitive accuracy and significantly reduced prediction times, with the proposed WGNO architecture reaching state-of-the-art performance. The presented neural operator has pronounced generalizing properties, meaning that for unseen problem parameters it delivers a solution accuracy close to that for parameters seen in the training dataset.
